posts.summary: This satirical piece parodies the popular 'Falsehoods Programmers Believe' format by applying it to dogs instead of technical topics. Rather than exposing edge cases in date handling or name validation, Wayne constructs a playful logical argument that all dogs are good dogs, all dogs are the best dogs, and nothing is better than dogs. The piece subverts reader expectations by using a technical writing format for pure wholesome content about canine appreciation.
key_points: Parodies the 'Falsehoods Programmers Believe' blog post genre that typically addresses technical edge cases
Constructs a mock-logical argument that leads to the conclusion all dogs are equally the best
Includes practical wisdom disguised as jokes (don't get a dog if you can't guarantee responsibility)
Uses the numbered list format to create escalating absurdity culminating in 'Dogs.'
money_quotes: Some dogs are bad dogs.
Okay, all dogs are good dogs, but there's only one best dog.
There are times when you shouldn't be petting a dog.
What about if you're a super spy and you have to choose between booping a nose and saving Wichita from an orbital laser cannon?
"Fuck Wichita?!"
I should get a dog if I can't guarantee 100% responsibility.
Programming is better than playing with dogs.
There is something better than dogs.
posts.key_insight: Sometimes the best use of a technical writing format is to remind us that all dogs are good dogs.

posts.summary: Doctests are a unique Python feature that embeds tests directly in documentation strings, simulating REPL interactions to verify examples work correctly. While they've fallen out of favor because they lack setup mechanisms and don't scale well compared to unit tests, Wayne argues they serve a fundamentally different purpose: detecting documentation bugs rather than code bugs. When a doctest fails but the unit test passes, it signals the documentation is out of sync with the code's actual behavior. Wayne sees doctests as an underexplored approach to keeping documentation consistent with code, inverting the common pattern of generating docs from code.
key_points: Doctests were invented in 1999 before TDD conventions existed, experimenting with embedding tests directly in documentation
They're rarely used today because they lack setup mechanisms like beforeEach and don't scale for complex testing scenarios
Doctests serve a different purpose than unit tests: they verify documentation accuracy rather than code correctness
When a doctest fails but unit tests pass, it reveals a documentation bug rather than a code bug
The approach of verifying human-written documentation against code is underexplored compared to auto-generating docs from code
money_quotes: Unit tests check your code for bugs, while doctests check your guide for bugs!
It's at the point where BDD advocates even call their tests 'specs', which is kind of like throwing a hammer at a car and calling the dent a blueprint.
Doctests go the other way. Instead of making the tests have documentation, it adds a tool to test documentation consistency.
Code-tests-code is much more flexible and scalable than documents-test-code.
Finding beauty in the missing places.
It's the dynamic nature, the tests as motion, that makes it an appealing substitute for documentation.
posts.key_insight: Doctests uniquely detect documentation bugs rather than code bugs—when they fail but unit tests pass, your docs are lying about what the code does.

posts.summary: This post demonstrates how to use TLA+ to model and verify a zero-downtime deployment system. Starting with a naive approach that immediately fails verification, Wayne iteratively builds a correct specification by adding a load balancer, update flags, and a coordinator process. The model checker exhaustively explores all possible orderings of concurrent server updates to find bugs. The final spec ensures both safety properties: at least one server is always accessible, and all accessible servers run the same code version.
key_points: TLA+ model checking exhaustively explores all possible orderings of concurrent operations - even 3 servers can have 90+ behaviors to verify
The naive deployment approach fails immediately because servers update at different rates, breaking version consistency
Adding a load balancer and coordinator process enables safe rolling updates by controlling which servers are accessible during updates
Temporal properties can verify system-wide conditions like 'when done, all servers must be back in the load balancer'
The spec can be extended to model failures, blue-green deployments, minimum capacity requirements, and lossy communication
money_quotes: As with any complicated system, it makes sense for us to design it up from, and then check the design itself for bugs.
We're done! Except that deployment model is horribly broken.
Instead of 6 behaviors and 18 steps, we have up to 90 behaviors and 1170 steps.
'But wait!' you've probably said, because you're a programmer and used to seeing things go horribly wrong.
Just kidding. We can totally check this in our model.
TLA+ has the power and flexibility to model all of these cases.
posts.key_insight: Formal specification lets you find concurrency bugs in deployment systems before writing any code by exhaustively checking all possible orderings of operations.

posts.summary: Wayne curates a list of programming articles that challenge mainstream beliefs, from skepticism about formal methods to defenses of PHP. He argues that good skeptical articles are rare because they require deep knowledge of both why people believe something and why it fails. The post establishes four criteria for quality skeptical writing: controversial stance, genuine understanding of the opposition, rigorous argumentation with data, and respectful tone. Wayne includes anti-examples for each criterion, including one of his own articles, demonstrating intellectual honesty about the difficulty of meeting these standards.
key_points: Skeptical articles are valuable precisely because they challenge assumptions that 'everybody believes' in programming
Good criticism requires understanding and being able to teach what you're criticizing - you can't effectively attack something you don't comprehend
Hard data and preemptive handling of objections are essential because skeptical articles face more scrutiny than mainstream ones
The author includes his own writing as an anti-example, noting embarrassment about a poorly-argued piece on testing private methods
Many canonical programming books are culturally important but rarely actually read, leading to distorted understanding of their ideas
money_quotes: A rough rule of thumb I have is that if you can't teach something to other people, you don't know it well enough to rant about it.
By definition you're writing something that a lot of people disagree with, so people are going to write responses. Don't give them any openings.
It's really hard to find good skeptical articles. It requires a deep knowledge of both why people believe something and why it breaks down.
Research is hard, writing is hard, and rhetoric is hard.
The article should treat the other side as reasonable, thoughtful people that just happen to be wrong.
I'm particularly interested in articles you disagree with, but still think are good.
posts.key_insight: Effective skepticism in programming requires not just disagreement but deep comprehension of opposing views, rigorous evidence, and enough intellectual honesty to acknowledge when your own work falls short.

posts.summary: The post introduces the concept of oracle functions in property-based testing with Hypothesis, demonstrating how known-correct functions can validate new implementations. Wayne presents four oracle patterns: using original functions during refactoring, leveraging special-case oracles that work on restricted domains, employing inefficient but correct implementations as references, and generating test inputs from expected outputs via reverse oracles. The approach complements traditional unit tests by providing broader coverage through randomized input generation. Wayne emphasizes that while oracles add testing complexity, they catch subtle bugs that might escape conventional tests.
key_points: Oracle functions are known-correct implementations you can compare against when testing new code, even if they seem redundant or impractical for production
Refactoring is an ideal oracle use case: your original working function validates the optimized version against edge cases you might miss
Special-case oracles work by restricting the input domain to cases where a simpler function is known to be correct
Reverse oracles flip the pattern by generating inputs from expected outputs, though they require careful construction to avoid test bugs
Property-based testing provides benefits orthogonal to unit tests, type systems, formal methods, and sleep—ideally you'd have all five
money_quotes: This may look like cheating ('if we already have a sorting function, why would we be writing another?'), but this can be a surprisingly powerful testing strategy.
We found this by using our original version as an oracle against the refactored version.
Over our restricted domain, the broken function is correct, and can be used as an oracle.
That might matter in production, but it won't matter too much in testing, so we can benefit from an oracle here.
This test ends up being more complicated than the others because it's easier for the test itself to have bugs.
This throws you down a rabbit hole ('I have to test my tests?!'), but you get much wider test coverage with PBTs in return for the extra complexity.
The benefits PBT provides are orthogonal to the benefits you get from unit tests, type systems, formal methods, and getting a full night's sleep.
posts.key_insight: Oracle functions let you leverage existing correct implementations—whether slow, partial, or soon-to-be-replaced—as test references, turning the apparent redundancy of 'testing against something you already have' into a powerful verification strategy.

posts.summary: The post examines the alarming consequences of bugs in scientific code, using the infamous Reinhart-Rogoff Excel error as a case study where a coding mistake influenced real austerity policies affecting Greece. Wayne argues that scientists lack the training and motivation to write rigorous code, yet their buggy analyses drive major policy decisions. He acknowledges that technical solutions exist but frames this fundamentally as a social problem—convincing non-programmers to adopt boring, frustrating practices for nebulous benefits. His tentative suggestion is mandatory code sharing with papers, though he admits this has significant practical barriers.
key_points: The Reinhart-Rogoff Excel bug led to austerity policies affecting entire countries, demonstrating the real-world stakes of scientific code quality
Even disciplined engineering teams with TDD and code reviews still ship serious bugs—researchers without any of these practices are far worse off
Scientists view coding as a necessary annoyance, not their actual job, which makes convincing them to adopt better practices extremely difficult
This is fundamentally a social problem, not a technical one, and engineers are notoriously bad at solving social problems
Requiring source code publication with papers could help but faces barriers like journal inconsistency and scientists' unfamiliarity with tools like git
money_quotes: Greece took on austerity because of a software bug. That's pretty fucked up.
Their job is research, not fighting weird computer bullshit.
How many bugs happen when you don't use any of that? Hell, how many bugs happen when you don't use functions, descriptive variables, or comments?
This is a social problem, not a technical one.
Watching engineers try to solve social problems is either tragic or hilarious, depending on how far you are from the explosion.
Being paranoid about science isn't fun.
posts.key_insight: Scientific code quality is a social problem masquerading as a technical one—we have the tools to write better code, but no good way to convince researchers to use them.

posts.summary: The post argues that examples in persuasive writing must match the writer's intent—instructive examples teach what something is, while persuasive examples demonstrate why it matters. Using a unit testing article as a case study, Wayne shows how a trivial 'add' function fails to convince skeptics because it doesn't reveal testing's actual benefits. He then constructs a better example using string parsing for citation coauthorship, demonstrating how tests catch subtle bugs like non-commutativity and edge cases that aren't obvious from reading code. The core message is that persuasive examples require significantly more craft than instructive ones.
key_points: Instructive examples show what and how; persuasive examples show why and why not—conflating them weakens arguments
Good persuasive examples must be simple without being trivial, complex without being convoluted, and balanced between abstract and concrete
The 'add numbers' unit test example fails persuasively because the function is obviously correct and the test is longer than the code
String processing makes excellent persuasive examples because everyone has been burned by it and it hides subtle bugs
Writing persuasive examples is harder and takes longer, but produces stronger arguments
money_quotes: The issue is that `add` is an instructive example, when what we want is a persuasive example.
Instructive examples show you what and how, while persuasive examples show you why and why not.
Without making that distinction clear, it's easy to accidentally write the former when you really need the latter.
A good persuasive example has to be simple without being trivial, complex without being convoluted, and just the right balance between general and specific.
Why should I bother to write test code if I can tell it's right by just looking at it?
After a while, there are too many edge cases to track, and you can no longer be confident about the correctness of your program by just looking at it.
Writing persuasive examples is hard. I think it's worth it, though, because it leads to a stronger article.
posts.key_insight: When trying to convince rather than merely explain, your examples must demonstrate why something matters, not just what it is—and achieving that balance of simplicity and complexity requires deliberate craft.

posts.summary: Hillel Wayne critiques Robert 'Uncle Bob' Martin's dismissive attitude toward software correctness techniques beyond unit testing. Martin argues that 'discipline' rather than tools is the solution to software problems, but Wayne contends this is naive—modern systems engineering recognizes that humans inevitably make mistakes, so we must design systems that catch errors rather than blame programmers. Wayne demonstrates through code examples how unit tests alone miss floating-point edge cases, null paths, and security vulnerabilities that other techniques like property-based testing, type systems, and static analysis would catch. The core argument is that no single technique is sufficient; we need every tool available because 'there's only one way a program is right and infinite ways a program can be wrong.'
key_points: Martin's advice to rely on 'discipline' instead of tools ignores that humans inevitably make mistakes—systems engineering focuses on making errors harder to make, easier to catch, or less damaging
Unit tests are insufficient because programmers can't anticipate all failure modes, as demonstrated by floating-point quirks, null paths through large codebases, and XSS vulnerabilities
Many techniques Martin dismisses as 'new' are actually older than unit testing—QuickCheck predates JUnit, TLA+ predates popular TDD by a decade
No single correctness technique is enough; type systems, contracts, formal specs, code review, and tests must all be combined
Martin systematically dismisses every correctness technique except unit tests: safer languages are the 'Dark Path', formal methods are 'shiny', type systems are unnecessary with test coverage
money_quotes: Uncle Bob is saying the solution for people writing bad code… is to not write bad code. Our programs would be perfect if it weren't for the programmers!

You can't rely on people to not fuck up on their own: after all, the US still has 30,000 auto deaths a year. Rather, the best way to reduce the volume and severity of mistakes is to adjust the system itself.
Don't just blame the drivers, give them safe roads! Give them seatbelts!
There's only one way a program is right and infinite ways a program can be wrong, and we can't assume that any tool we use will prevent more than a narrow slice of all those wrong ways.
By being so dismissive of everything but unit tests, he's actively discouraging us from using our whole range of techniques. He demands we run blind and blames us for tripping.
QuickCheck is older than JUnit. TLA+ is almost a decade older than popular TDD. If anything, unit testing is the 'new and shiny' thing!
We'll continue to struggle with our bugs, curse our model checkers, and make software engineering just a little bit better. We don't believe in silver bullets.
posts.key_insight: Software correctness requires combining every available technique—unit tests, type systems, static analysis, formal methods—because humans inevitably make mistakes and no single tool catches more than a narrow slice of possible errors.

posts.summary: Wayne argues provocatively that unit tests shouldn't be classified as 'tests' but rather as 'development' activities. His reasoning centers on emergence: while unit tests verify individual components work in isolation, programs exhibit complex behaviors arising from component interactions that unit tests cannot capture. Since unit tests don't verify the actual program behaves correctly—only that isolated code units do—they belong to the development phase rather than the testing phase. This reframing has implications for how we think about Agile vs Waterfall, the role of QA engineers, and what real testing should look like.
key_points: Code exhibits emergence like physical systems—the complexity of a program vastly exceeds the sum of its well-tested units
Unit tests verify code behavior, not program behavior, making them development scaffolding rather than actual tests
Reframing unit tests as development makes Agile and Waterfall methodologies converge rather than contradict
Without unit tests counting as 'tested,' teams must explore more sophisticated testing approaches like visual snapshots, state machines, and fault trees
QA engineering deserves recognition as genuinely difficult specialist work, not second-class citizenship
money_quotes: Enough interacting units and your program is vastly more complex than the sum of its parts.

Since you're interested in the program behaving correctly, not its individual units of code, and since unit tests don't determine the program is behaving correctly (only the code), they're not testing the program. Ergo, they're not tests. They're development.
You can no longer point at a codebase swathed in unit tests and say 'yeah that's tested.'
Good testing is really, really hard. In many cases it's harder than slinging the business logic.
QA engineers aren't second-class citizens, they're specialists.
Saying dumb shit has never stopped me before so let's do some grambling!
posts.key_insight: Unit tests verify that code units work in isolation, but since programs exhibit emergent complexity from component interactions, unit tests are better understood as development scaffolding than as actual tests of program correctness.

posts.summary: Hillel Wayne responds to Eric Smith's 'Why TDD is Crap' talk, systematically addressing each criticism while maintaining that TDD is likely a useful correctness technique. He examines the limited empirical evidence (notably the Nagappan study showing 60% defect reduction at 25% time cost), acknowledges TDD's limitations, and argues it should be part of a broader correctness toolkit alongside contracts, property-based testing, and upfront design. The post emphasizes that comparing TDD to 'nothing' is the wrong framing—we should compare it to other correctness techniques, each with tradeoffs. Wayne concludes that while we don't know much about what good software engineering looks like, TDD probably helps, and we shouldn't mock those who disagree with our preferred techniques.
key_points: The Nagappan study found TDD projects had roughly 60% fewer defects but took 25% longer—suggesting net benefit when factoring maintenance
Comparing any technique to 'nothing' is a discussion mistake; we should compare techniques to each other, each with their own maintenance overhead
Property-based testing and design-by-contract complement rather than replace TDD—they catch different classes of bugs
Upfront design (not BDUF, but things like sequence diagrams) catches errors that are vastly harder to fix in code than in design
The lack of hard data on TDD reflects how difficult software engineering research is, not TDD's ineffectiveness
money_quotes: Just because Linus Torvalds didn't need TDD doesn't mean that you and I, who aren't anywhere as good, don't need it either.
Fact is, we're all mediocre at best, and we should be choosing our techniques on what we need, not what programming legends need.
Failsafes can cause critical failures in large systems, but the failsafes are the reason your system can afford to grow large in the first place.
But if you put a gun to my head and asked if TDD worked, I'd probably say 'yes'.
That's a common discussion mistake we make: comparing 'something' to 'nothing' when we really should be comparing it to 'something else'.
Testing does not substitute for thinking.
Most existing correctness techniques are miserable to use. If you don't hate it, you haven't pushed it hard enough.
If your only takeaway from this essay is 'learn mermaid', I've done my job.
posts.key_insight: TDD is likely useful but should be part of a diverse correctness toolkit, and we should compare techniques to each other rather than to doing nothing.

posts.summary: Hillel Wayne demonstrates how to calculate personal burn rates using J, an array programming language, as he prepares to leave work for EMT training. The post walks through increasingly complex financial calculations: simple division for basic burn rate, tables for comparing multiple scenarios, and rank-3 arrays for tracking savings over time with variable income. He shows how J's native support for arrays and rank operations makes these multi-dimensional calculations concise compared to traditional languages. The post concludes with a reflection on how J represents a 'Specific Purpose Language' optimized for numerical computation rather than general programming tasks.
key_points: J's array-first design eliminates the need for explicit loops or maps when operating on multiple values simultaneously
The table adverb (/) creates multi-dimensional arrays from operations on arrays of different lengths
Rank conjunction (f"n) allows applying operations to specific dimensional slices of higher-rank arrays
J trades common language features like dictionaries for specialized array manipulation capabilities
The language occupies a middle ground between general-purpose and domain-specific languages
money_quotes: If you do a binary operator on two equal length arrays, it will operate elementwise.
One thing J taught me is just how many tradeoffs are in a programming language. Not just at the level of performance, safety, or semantics, but on a 'what is this language for' level.
J doesn't have a lot of 'common' language features, like dictionaries or sane conditionals. Instead it has things like first-class array manipulation and simplex generators.
I wouldn't want to use it for web scraping or text munging, but it's now my favorite tool for calculations.
It's a sort of hybrid of a general purpose language and a DSL. Let's call it a Specific Purpose Language, where the purpose in this case is 'doing stuff to numbers'.
posts.key_insight: Array programming languages like J represent a 'Specific Purpose Language' paradigm that trades general-purpose features for exceptional expressiveness in numerical computation.

posts.summary: This post builds up the concept of contract programming from first principles, distinguishing it from type checking and unit testing. Starting with a simple number-guessing game, Wayne demonstrates how preconditions and postconditions can enforce correctness at function boundaries. He argues that while Python's assert statements are crude, they capture the essential idea: contracts specify what callers must guarantee and what functions promise in return. The post surveys contract support across languages, from inadequate library-based solutions to D's built-in support to Eiffel's gold-standard implementation, ultimately encouraging developers to make implicit contracts explicit even without native language support.
key_points: Contracts are distinct from type systems and tests - they act on real-world dynamic behavior and can express relationships between parameters that types cannot
Preconditions specify caller responsibilities, postconditions specify function guarantees, and together they localize bugs to specific boundaries
Most contract libraries fail by treating contracts as type checkers or using limited DSLs instead of the full host language
Class invariants (checked before and after every method) simplify the inheritance vs composition decision
Even crude assert statements are valuable - most codebases have implicit contracts that should be made explicit
money_quotes: My favorite definition of assert is 'a conditional which, if false, indicates a bug in your program.'
It conceptually separates anticipated bad behavior from impossible behavior. You handle the former with guard statements, and the latter with contracts.
Contracts make pretty bad type systems. You can dismiss it as 'like a static type system, but worse' or as 'I didn't need types anyway, why bother with contracts?'
Just as contracts make for poor types, types make for poor contracts.
Unlike catching any other kind of error it's so obviously and unambiguously a fatal error that catching it is a war crime.
Most large codebases have a lot of implicit contracts 'enforced' through unit tests and guard statements. Might as well make them explicit.
posts.key_insight: Contracts occupy a distinct correctness niche from types and tests by expressing runtime invariants and inter-parameter relationships at function boundaries, making implicit assumptions explicit and localizing bugs to their source.

posts.summary: The post introduces a simple visual notation of 'boxes, arrows, and ports' to analyze software architecture, revealing that classes and microservices are topologically equivalent from a design perspective. Wayne argues that the key difference is the size of the largest 'proper interface' - Ruby's biggest enforced boundary is the class, while microservices enforce boundaries at the service level via HTTP APIs. He concludes that microservices' separation of concerns is essentially using devops to compensate for programming language limitations, and that choosing microservices primarily for this benefit is misguided.
key_points: Classes and microservices have the same topological representation when abstracted to boxes, arrows, and ports - the difference is enforcement
Ruby's module system lacks proper interfaces (only conventions), making it fragile and prone to coupling violations
Microservices provide larger proper interfaces than classes, but 'federated microservices' that know about each other recreate the same coupling problems
Convention-based interfaces fail because programmers must know about them, they're hard to document at scale, violations are easy to make accidentally or intentionally
The desire for separation of concerns through microservices reveals a deficiency in programming languages rather than an inherent advantage of distributed systems
money_quotes: Abstract far enough and classes and microservices have the same representation!
Convention, as we all know, is fragile.
Eventually, the system becomes entangled and everything becomes terrible.
One of the major claimed benefits of a microservice architecture, separation of concerns, is us using devops to compensate for a flaw in our programming language.
Without a common notation for boxes, ports, and interfaces, there's no easy way to communicate what, exactly, it is you're losing.
Use `send` and your coworkers will throw rocks at you.
posts.key_insight: Microservices and classes are structurally identical - microservices only provide better separation of concerns because they enforce interfaces that programming languages fail to enforce.

posts.summary: The post challenges the conventional wisdom that comments should only explain 'why' and not 'what'. Wayne argues that the real issue is context switching—whether that's looking up variable meanings, digging through git blame, or jumping between extracted methods. He critiques the 'Extract Till You Drop' style of clean code, showing how aggressive method extraction can actually harm readability by forcing readers to jump around. The core argument is that keeping information where it's needed—sometimes via 'what' comments—reduces cognitive load better than either cryptic code or over-extracted methods.
key_points: Looking things up is the core problem—whether it's variable names, commit messages, or extracted methods, context switches harm understanding
The 'comments should explain why not what' rule is too absolute; sometimes what-comments prevent dangerous assumptions
Aggressive method extraction (Clean Code style) can make code harder to follow by spreading logic across many small methods
Information should live where you need it, whether that's descriptive names, comments, or inline code
Comments are not a substitute for clean code, but they can complement it when extraction would fragment understanding
money_quotes: Both of these cases share the same problem: looking things up is hard.
Best case it's a context switch that takes time away from understanding the problem. Worst case you don't look it up and make a potentially-dangerous assumption.
So much better, right?! `replace` is now two lines instead of ten. But now there's six other methods you have to read to understand how the class works.
Is that really so much better than using comments?
I'm just saying that there are at least a few cases where writing a 'what' comment is the right choice, so we shouldn't reject them out-of-hand.
posts.key_insight: The real enemy of code readability isn't comments or extracted methods—it's context switching, and sometimes a 'what' comment is the most direct way to keep information where it's needed.

posts.summary: The post examines what it actually means to choose 'the right tool for the job' by analyzing different ways we evaluate technology choices. Wayne walks through a hierarchy of evidence: requirements, personal experience, authority, abstract arguments, examples, case studies, and formal research. He argues that personal experience and authority are unreliable due to cognitive biases, while abstract arguments often cancel each other out. Case studies emerge as the practical gold standard, being more rigorous than examples but more accessible than formal research. The conclusion is that laypeople can and should produce case studies from their own work to help the community make better decisions.
key_points: Personal experience and authority are poor guides because of cognitive biases and limited frames of reference - we're bad at objectively judging ourselves
Abstract arguments are unreliable because opposing sides can construct equally plausible reasoning, leaving 'the best writer' to win
Examples are valuable but can be rigged by choosing which aspects of reality to represent and which to omit
Case studies are the practical gold standard because they faithfully represent real tradeoffs and can't be easily argued away
Formal research is ideal but rarely available for specific technology questions due to the difficulty and cost of software engineering research
money_quotes: I don't think anybody wants to use the wrong tool for the job, unless they're trying to sabotage it.
We're bad at objectively judging ourselves. There's only a weak correlation between how much you believe you understand something and how much you actually do.
If all you've got is 'reason', the Right Tool may be 'whichever has the best marketing.'
I think a good example is worth a hundred abstract arguments.
The writer can choose which parts of the real world they reproduce and this, unconsciously or not, can rig the evidence.
Case studies are some of the strongest evidence that the average person can produce, because they can faithfully represent all of the tradeoffs in a tool.
You're presumably doing something at work, and it's usually possible to turn 'here's what I did this quarter' into something people can learn from.
posts.key_insight: Case studies are the most practical form of rigorous evidence for technology decisions because they represent real tradeoffs and, unlike formal research, can be produced by working engineers from their everyday experiences.

posts.summary: The post demonstrates how combining property-based testing (PBT) with contracts can create effective integration tests. Contracts define preconditions and postconditions that serve as PBT invariants, eliminating the need to manually specify test assertions. When PBT generates inputs matching preconditions, contracts automatically verify correctness throughout the call chain. The author shows this through a list removal example and a siteswap validator, illustrating how contracts 'chain' through function calls to provide integration-level verification. This approach addresses PBT's challenge of coming up with invariants and integration testing's complexity of orchestrating components.
key_points: Contracts and PBT have complementary tradeoffs: contracts need wide testing to confirm them, while PBT needs good invariants—combining them solves both problems
The PBT test acts as a fuzzer that generates inputs while contracts serve as the invariants, requiring no manual assertions in tests
Contract checking 'chains' through function calls, so testing a high-level function automatically verifies contracts of all called functions
Precondition failures in directly-called functions usually indicate test errors, while postcondition failures indicate implementation bugs
This technique transforms the challenge of writing integration tests into the simpler task of writing contracts on individual functions
money_quotes: Maybe they complement each other: 'your code doesn't violate any contracts' counts as a PBT invariant.
Note we've tested our function without ever specifying what we're checking in the test! All it's doing is acting as a fuzzer.
Combining contracts and PBT gives us integration tests!
The test doesn't have to know anything about what siteswap_to_balls does, only that it accepts lists of integers. The contracts do the rest.
Given that one of the big challenges in PBT is coming up with invariants and one of the challenges of integration testing is the integration, I think this idea is worth exploring.
posts.key_insight: Using contracts as PBT invariants creates self-documenting integration tests where the contracts do the verification work and testing becomes pure fuzzing.

posts.summary: This post explains why Vim's macro system is uniquely powerful: macros are simply stored keystrokes in registers, giving them complete access to all Vim functionality. Wayne provides practical techniques for recording, editing, and debugging macros, along with useful settings and commands that enhance macro usage. He covers marks, registers, visual mode, and conditionals within macros, while cautioning that complex recursive macros often indicate you should use a different tool entirely. The post serves as a practical companion to Vim's sparse official macro documentation.
key_points: Macros are just keystrokes stored in registers, which means they can be pasted, edited, and manipulated like any text string
When you make a mistake recording a macro, undo it and continue—then manually remove the mistake and undo from the stored keystrokes afterward
Starting macros with a guard prefix (like ^) and ending with a setup suffix (like j) makes them more deterministic and repeatable
Marks and registers can be used within macros to track state across complex operations
If you need conditionals or recursion in a macro, you've probably chosen the wrong tool—consider functions, external filters, or regex substitutions instead
money_quotes: Macros are just stored keystrokes. When you record a macro, it's saved to the appropriate register, and it can be pasted and appended like any other saved string.
Kind of a shame they're so neglected. The vim doc has what, fifty lines devoted to macros?
In replay mode, an undo will undo the entire macro up until that point, erasing all of your hard work and bleeding the macro out into the rest of your text.
The more deterministic the macro, the better.
If you need a conditional in your macro you should probably be writing a function instead.
Macros are best for simple ad-hoc repetitive edits.
Writing recursive macros usually means you picked the wrong tool for the job.
Just write a function dangit
posts.key_insight: Vim macros derive their power from being plain text keystrokes stored in registers, making them both infinitely flexible and trivially editable—but knowing when not to use them is just as important as knowing how.

posts.summary: Wayne highlights an obscure software engineering paper about abbreviated vs. full-word identifier names as exemplary research methodology. Despite its mundane-sounding topic, the paper demonstrates exceptional rigor: a comprehensive literature review, mixed qualitative and quantitative methods, objective defect measurement through deliberately seeded faults, and meticulous experimental design that addresses nearly every threat to validity. The researchers tested 100 participants across experience levels, used real codebases, and followed up with ethnographic observation. The surprising finding—no significant difference between abbreviated and full names—is made credible by the methodology's thoroughness.
key_points: Good SE research requires narrowing scope to answerable questions rather than vague concepts like 'clean code'
The paper combines quantitative experiments with qualitative ethnography, addressing the field's overvaluation of purely quantitative methods
Objective measurement of 'defects' was achieved by deliberately inserting known faults into existing codebases
The experimental design systematically eliminated threats to validity: large sample size, mixed experience levels, real codebases, controlled for fatigue and familiarity
The unexpected null result (no difference between naming styles) becomes trustworthy precisely because of the rigorous methodology
money_quotes: We need hard numbers to answer research questions, sure, but we need quals to know what questions we should even be asking.

In Leprechauns of Software Engineering Laurent Bossavit talks about how most people use words like 'errors' and 'quality' without rigorously defining it, so we have no way of knowing what we're actually seriously measuring.
We tend to overvalue quantitative methods (controlled experiments, data mining) and undervalue qualitative methods (interviews, ethnographies, tailing people).
Literature reviews are really hard to do and they aced it.
Ultimately, they found no significant difference between abbreviated and full names. Unexpected, but the care they put into this reason makes me completely willing to trust them.
posts.key_insight: The value of software engineering research lies not in exciting conclusions but in methodological rigor that makes any conclusion—even a null result—trustworthy.

posts.summary: This post demonstrates how to use Alloy, a lightweight formal methods tool, to verify that randomized game configurations are always winnable. Using Link to the Past Randomizer as an example, Wayne builds an Alloy model that represents chests, items, and their requirements, then formally proves that certain placement restrictions guarantee the game can be completed. The post progressively refines the model to handle increasingly complex scenarios: unique item placement, requirement cycles, multi-item requirements, and alternative puzzle solutions. Wayne concludes by comparing Alloy favorably to TLA+ for its simplicity and speed, while noting its limitations for dynamic systems and concurrency.
key_points: Alloy translates models into SAT problems, making it extremely fast (under 150ms) but limited to bounded model checking
The 'trace' pattern solves Alloy's lack of recursion support by modeling reachability as a sequence of inventory states
Relations in Alloy are just tuples of atoms, allowing bidirectional traversal and powerful set operations like transitive closure
Alloy excels at verifying relational data structures but struggles with dynamic systems, concurrency, and liveness properties
Forcing certain critical items to be 'free' (not in any chest) can guarantee winnability when simpler restrictions fail
money_quotes: We can check with unit tests, but tests don't give us 100% certainty. If we want that, we need to use formal methods.
For a verifier, Alloy is pretty damn fast!
Here's something very, very important about Alloy: it does not support multisets.
Another important thing: Alloy doesn't like recursion.
Instead of making it impossible to lose, our restrictions made it impossible to win.
Alloy's simplicity is also a weakness. It's pretty tuned to inspecting relationships in data structures. The further you get from that, the harder things get.
It's not as flexible or powerful as other formal methods tools, but it's fun and you can get up and going pretty quickly.
posts.key_insight: Alloy's relational algebra foundation makes it ideal for quickly verifying properties of data structures and item relationships, even if the problem domain (game randomizers) isn't its intended niche.

posts.summary: This post introduces a curated list of important women in computer science beyond the commonly cited figures of Grace Hopper, Margaret Hamilton, and Ada Lovelace. Wayne establishes criteria for inclusion: specific identifiable contributions, technical (not social/political) achievements, and historical work predating 1997. The post serves as an introduction to a sampler list rather than an exhaustive compilation, acknowledging the author's limitations in covering contributions outside his specialties.
key_points: The same few women (Hopper, Hamilton, Lovelace) are repeatedly cited while many others are overlooked
Contributions must be specific, technical, and foundational to make the list
The 1997 cutoff is deliberately arbitrary ('the last time Britain won Eurovision')
The list is explicitly a sampler, not comprehensive, due to the author's admitted laziness and specialty limitations
money_quotes: I'm tired of hearing about Grace Hopper, Margaret Hamilton, and Ada Lovelace. Can't we think of someone *else* for once?
I made exceptions when I felt like it because I can.
orphan work that's disconnected to our modern hellscape of a field
I left a lot of qualified people out because there were a lot of people and I got lazy.
posts.key_insight: The tech industry's habit of citing only three women when discussing CS history obscures the many other women who made foundational technical contributions to the field.

posts.summary: The author discusses pedagogical challenges when teaching TLA+ through the PlusCal abstraction layer. While PlusCal simplifies syntax significantly, certain advanced concepts like temporal invariants require underlying TLA+ knowledge that isn't otherwise needed in PlusCal. Rather than either using clunky workarounds or dumping inapplicable concepts on readers, the author explores a third approach: finding ways to make each prerequisite concept independently useful within PlusCal contexts, then synthesizing them later when needed.
key_points: PlusCal abstracts away TLA+'s most convoluted syntax while preserving most of the learning, making it ideal for teaching
Some invariants (like 'x never decreases') are trivial in pure TLA+ but require ugly bookkeeping hacks in PlusCal
The pedagogical dilemma: teach concepts that aren't immediately useful, or use inferior workarounds
The solution is to find natural contexts where each prerequisite concept becomes useful on its own, enabling later synthesis
Stuttering can be taught through crash simulation, primed variables through debugging error traces
money_quotes: I asked my technical reviewer to be 'almost-comically brutal' and he's risen to the challenge.
PlusCal only abstracts away setting and updating values, which happens to be the most convoluted part of TLA+.
I don't want to sledgehammer the reader with information that only makes sense much later.
This works, but it has two major problems... We've added another variable to track solely to help with the bookkeeping.
We make things cumbersome or we could write `[][y => x' >= x]_x`. No bookkeeping needed at all.
Either we use a shitty workaround or we bite the bullet and teach a bunch of inapplicable concepts to get to the good stuff.
While all these concepts are unnecessary in Pluscal, I can find ways to make them useful.
Teaching is hard!!!
posts.key_insight: When teaching abstractions that leak, the solution isn't to dump prerequisites or accept inferior workarounds, but to find independent contexts where each prerequisite becomes naturally useful before synthesis is needed.

posts.summary: Hillel Wayne responds to Tomas Petricek's argument that modern programming language theory makes restrictive assumptions that prevent us from seeing alternative paradigms. Wayne argues that Petricek's two proposed 'unthinkable' paradigms—taxonomic programming and metaphorical programming—are not actually unthinkable at all. He demonstrates that taxonomic programming was extensively explored during the OOP 'method wars' of 1980-1995 and was formalized through contracts and Hoare logic. Metaphorical programming, he argues, exists in APL languages (which use primitives to shape thought) and in DSLs like Inform 7. His conclusion is that these paradigms aren't incommensurate alternatives—they're already present in programming, just underappreciated.
key_points: OOP's 'method wars' (1980-1995) produced over 50 different taxonomic approaches with rich relationship types beyond just 'implements' and 'is-a', eventually consolidating into UML
Kent's Law (formulated twice): all nontrivial classification systems ban useful categories, and all taxonomies have horrible edge cases because reality is too messy to categorize
APL languages embody metaphorical programming by using primitives to shape thought—Iverson believed our modes of thinking are based on available tools
DSLs like Inform 7, LabVIEW, and AutoHotKey are deeply metaphorical but get less theoretical attention because they're 'applied' and not aimed at programmers
Contracts in OOP (formalized via Hoare logic) show how taxonomic relationships can be mathematically rigorous
money_quotes: All nontrivial classification systems inevitably ban some useful categories.
All taxonomies have horrible edge cases.
Reality is way too messy to categorize. All people have a birthday? Does 'King Arthur' have a birthday? Is that even a useful question?
APLs are considered functional programming languages, which seems to me an admission that nobody really knows what's going on with them.
Iverson believed that our modes of thought were based on the tools we had.
Taxonomic and metaphorical programming are already present, we just don't pay attention to them.
DSLs are less respectable for some bizarre reason. Which is a huge shame: a lot of mindbending, interesting, and useful languages are DSLs.
posts.key_insight: The 'unthinkable' programming paradigms that critics claim we're missing have already been thought and implemented—we just don't recognize them because they don't fit our narrative of what counts as serious computer science.

posts.summary: This post demonstrates how to model Redux's state management pattern using TLA+, a formal specification language. Wayne walks through creating a reducer specification, then proves it 'refines' a simpler global state model by showing both systems always end up with equivalent state. He also shows how to create a temporal property that ensures all state changes go through Redux's proper channels, catching violations even when integrating with other components. The post concludes with the observation that specification helped Wayne understand Redux better than tutorials did.
key_points: Redux's action-reduce-update cycle can be modeled as a single atomic step in TLA+ since there's no concurrency between reduction and update
Refinement proofs let you verify that a complex implementation behaves equivalently to a simpler abstract model
Temporal properties can enforce that state changes only happen through designated channels, catching violations automatically
PlusCal specs cannot import other PlusCal specs with actions, requiring TLA+ for component composition
The process of formal specification often improves understanding of a system more than reading tutorials
money_quotes: Careful refinement is one of the precious few ways we know of to prove code correct at scale.
Comprehension is one of the major benefits of specification; it's just less visceral than formally verifying properties.
Redux is usually presented as a pretty complicated topic to 'get', but the JS folk I talked to said this spec mostly matches how Redux is supposed to work.
The component is able to show that something else updated the global state without going through its channels.
We do this to blueprint our system and find errors in our designs, not to confirm the code itself works.
TLA+ is not only able to identify that the equivalence invariant has failed, it's able to identify the series of steps that leads to the invariant failing.
posts.key_insight: Formal specification is valuable not just for verification but for understanding—modeling Redux in TLA+ revealed its essential structure more clearly than tutorials could.

posts.summary: The post argues that programmers cannot reason their way to answers about complex debates like static vs dynamic typing because software development is a system with countless interconnected impacts. Direct impacts lead to linear first-order effects, which cascade into second-order effects, and everything becomes nonlinear when factors change the rate at which other factors affect outcomes. The author contends that the human mind simply isn't powerful enough to trace all these cascading effects, comparing the challenge to the unsolvable three-body problem in physics. While humans develop useful heuristics and intuitions, these are context-dependent and don't generalize well. The solution proposed is empirical software engineering: running controlled studies to measure actual outcomes rather than reasoning from first principles.
key_points: Software development is a complex system where everything impacts everything else through chains of direct, linear, and nonlinear effects
Second-order and higher-order impacts often work against direct impacts, making intuitive reasoning unreliable
Human heuristics work well within narrow personal experience but fail when generalized to others' contexts
Both sides of debates like static vs dynamic typing can construct plausible chains of impacts supporting their position
Empirical software engineering—controlled studies measuring actual outcomes—is the only way to answer these questions definitively
money_quotes: Our minds are just not powerful enough.
Understanding second-order impacts is incredibly unintuitive.
Are your build times slow because you're using Chef or because you bought the spinny office chairs? Who knows!
If you disagree, consider physics: there's no closed-form solution to the three body problem. Why would a 100-body problem be any different?
Just because TDD works for you doesn't mean it's the right choice for everyone else.
Software is the richest industry in the history of the world, and it's absolutely crazy to think we can't afford basic research on how to write software good.
We use our reason and experience to guide what we choose to investigate, but ultimately trust the data over our own experiences.
posts.key_insight: Complex software debates cannot be settled by reasoning alone because cascading impacts through interconnected systems exceed human cognitive capacity—only empirical studies can reveal actual outcomes.

posts.summary: Hillel Wayne argues that the biggest problem with APL-family languages isn't their cryptic symbols or write-only reputation, but discoverability—finding the right primitive among hundreds of options is extremely difficult. Using J as an example, he shows how a 22-character solution took 40 minutes to write because he had to search through 200 primitives to find the right combination. He proposes three solutions: labeling primitives by complexity level, categorizing them by problem domain, and creating a library of reusable components and idioms. These changes would make APLs more learnable and code more readable by letting programmers recognize common patterns.
key_points: APL's real problem isn't symbols or readability—it's the difficulty of discovering which primitives to use for a given task
J has about 200 primitives ranging from basic operations to extremely specialized functions like Mealy machine partitioning, all presented without hierarchy
A component library of reusable idioms would be more useful than a phrasebook of complete solutions to specific problems
Primitives should be labeled by complexity (basic/advanced/expert) and by problem domain (filtering, shape manipulation, etc.)
Semantic highlighting of recognized idioms could improve both discoverability and readability
money_quotes: That's 22 characters. It's fast, clean, and took 40 damn minutes to write.
To find the right primitive I have to review 200 of them.
J has a phrasebook that's supposed to kind of fill this role, but it gives you complete solutions to specific problems, not reusable pieces you can build with.
I'm sure a J expert can look at this and say 'no you're supposed to use the Foobar primitive which makes it trivial', but that's my whole point.
The `;:` dyad, which partitions y based on a specified Mealy machine.
Instead of 40 minutes, it takes 5.
posts.key_insight: APL languages need better organization of their primitives—by complexity level, problem domain, and reusable idioms—to transform them from powerful-but-opaque tools into learnable, practical languages.

posts.summary: This satirical post presents absurd explanations of basic programming concepts in the style of bad monad tutorials. Wayne demonstrates how explanations can fail by being either overly abstract and mathematical, using bizarre analogies, oversimplifying to the point of inaccuracy, dismissing the importance of understanding, or being needlessly jargon-heavy. The post serves as a critique of how programming education often goes wrong, using the notorious difficulty of monad tutorials as its framing device.
key_points: Bad explanations come in many flavors: too abstract, too analogical, too dismissive, or too jargon-heavy
Analogies that don't map to the actual concept (tricycles and pizza for loops) are worse than no analogy at all
Oversimplifying concepts to 'you already do this' erases important distinctions that matter
Dismissing topics as unimportant ('just make everything one big file') undermines learning
Dense jargon without unpacking ('Hoare refinement of a modular term algebra') excludes rather than explains
money_quotes: Mathematics doesn't have a sense of time, but often we need to track time... The benefits of this should be obvious.
So a loop is just like a tricycle and the body is a pizza. Does that make sense?
A function is just a way to copy-paste code without needing to hit 'paste' a whole lot.
There's some important theoretical reasons here, but don't worry about them. You care much more about the actual use of them, which is looking good in a whiteboard interview.
Most people can get away just fine with making everything one big file.
A class is just the Hoare refinement of a modular term algebra, what's the problem?
posts.key_insight: The many ways monad tutorials fail—overwrought formalism, strained analogies, dismissive oversimplification—are the same ways we fail to explain any programming concept.

posts.summary: The post addresses a common complaint about learning TLA+: the lack of accessible examples between trivial toy problems and complex industrial algorithms. Wayne explains this gap exists because TLA+ is typically used to design complicated systems, often proprietary ones, and the community is very small. He introduces a curated list of beginner-to-moderate complexity industrial examples, excluding those already in standard resources. The post serves as a practical resource for learners seeking real-world TLA+ specifications.
key_points: The hardest part of learning TLA+ is finding good examples at the right complexity level
Most existing examples are either toy problems or immensely complex algorithms
The TLA+ community is extremely small, limiting the pool of example producers
Proprietary usage means many real-world specifications are never shared publicly
Wayne curates a list of 'industrial' examples at beginner-to-moderate complexity
money_quotes: People always tell me that the hardest part of learning TLA+ is finding good examples.
Most of the main ones out there are either toy problems or immensely complex algorithms.
If you're using it, you're trying to design something complicated, and that's usually because you're trying to *sell* something complicated.
You could probably fit all of the TLA+ experts in the world in a small coffee shop.
Not a whole lot of people producing examples + most examples being proprietary = not enough to learn from.
posts.key_insight: TLA+ suffers from an example gap—real-world usage tends to be proprietary and complex, leaving learners without accessible intermediate examples to bridge the gap from toys to production specifications.

posts.summary: Hillel Wayne uses the Alloy specification language to formally model package managers and demonstrate why Nix's 'purely functional' approach solves real problems. He starts with a naive package manager that fails when upgrading shared dependencies, then shows how allowing multiple versions prevents breakage. The key insight comes when modeling the gap between developer and user environments: traditional package managers can fail because developers don't explicitly track all transitive dependencies. Nix solves this by coupling each requirement to its specific program, creating isolated dependency trees that guarantee reproducible builds across machines.
key_points: A naive package manager that only allows one version of a package breaks when upgrading shared dependencies between programs
Simply allowing multiple versions installed simultaneously fixes the upgrade problem but doesn't solve the developer-user environment mismatch
Traditional package managers fail when developers have implicit dependencies already installed that users lack
Nix's approach of isolating requirements per-program guarantees that explicit dependencies always match actual requirements
Formal specification revealed the 'upgrades are tricky' problem within minutes of starting, demonstrating how structured thinking about systems improves quality
money_quotes: Buckle up everyone, it's time for some formal methods.
Predicates don't set values. They're just statements that are true or false.
Since we're installing requirements as coupled to the appropriate program, she must install a complete copy of every requirement in order to get the program.
By keeping all of the requirements isolated, you have a deterministic build. If it works on your machine, it should (ideally) work on other machines regardless of their current state.
Just having a way to clearly think about your system makes a huge difference in the overall quality.
The 'upgrades are tricky' case came up within a few minutes of me starting the spec.
I'm not investing 50+ hours into understanding package management problems for a 2,000 word post.
posts.key_insight: Formal specification isn't just for finding bugs—it's a powerful tool for understanding problem domains, as modeling a package manager quickly reveals why Nix's isolated dependency approach solves real reproducibility problems.

posts.summary: This post provides a comprehensive taxonomy of automated testing approaches, dividing them into automanual tests (where humans specify exact inputs and outputs) and generative tests (where programs search for failing inputs). Wayne walks through unit tests, integration tests, acceptance tests, feature tests, diff tests, and parameterized tests in the automanual category, then covers property tests, fuzz tests, and model tests in the generative category. He argues that while automanual tests are easier to write and deterministic, they only test tiny slivers of state space, whereas generative tests explore much wider ranges at the cost of specificity. The piece serves as both reference guide and gentle critique of testing orthodoxy.
key_points: Automanual tests specify exact inputs and outputs; generative tests let programs search for failing inputs across broader state spaces
The distinction between unit and integration tests is fuzzy, but integration tests are those that might fail due to emergence—complex behavior from interacting components
Property testing checks invariants across generated inputs and uses shrinking to find minimal failing cases
Model-based testing generalizes property testing by modeling tests as state machines that can choose their own transitions
Feature tests (like Cucumber) try to be tests, specifications, and documentation simultaneously, which creates tension since these have different requirements
money_quotes: I think a good difference is that unit tests are unaffected by emergence, or complex behavior that arises from multiple interacting components.
Emergence is a hellishly complicated problem and many, many bugs hide in the interactions between units.
Calling integration tests 'contract tests' is completely missing the point of contracts.
Once we get to the level of 'product' we're dealing with reality and reality is pretty damn hard to test.
Feature tests simultaneously try to be tests, specifications, and documents. Unfortunately, all three of these have different requirements.
While automanual tests give you complete information about a single input, generative tests only give you partial information on a range of inputs.
Finding good invariants to test can be a very difficult problem.
Mutation tests rerun your other tests on randomly modified versions of the code: if your tests still pass, they're probably broken.
posts.key_insight: The fundamental tradeoff in testing is between automanual tests that give complete information about specific cases and generative tests that give partial information across much wider state spaces.

posts.summary: The post traces the historical evolution of the equals sign as an assignment operator, starting from the four dominant languages of the early 1960s (COBOL, FORTRAN, ALGOL-60, and LISP). While ALGOL and its descendants used ':=' for assignment and '=' for equality, the shift began with CPL's introduction of '=' for initialized definitions, continued through BCPL's simplifications, and culminated in B where Ken Thompson chose '=' for all assignment purely because it's shorter than ':='. C inherited this convention, and most modern languages follow C's lead despite ':=' being the more historically common and semantically clearer choice.
key_points: ALGOL-60 and most of its descendants used ':=' for assignment and '=' for equality, which was the dominant convention
CPL introduced '=' for initialization to support three different initialization modes (by value, reference, and substitution)
Ken Thompson chose '=' for assignment in B specifically because it's one character shorter, reasoning that assignment is twice as common as equality testing
The '==' operator for equality was invented as a workaround to disambiguate after '=' was repurposed for assignment
Most correctness-oriented languages (Pascal, Eiffel, Ada, ML) continued using ':=' for assignment
money_quotes: A common FP critique of imperative programming goes like this: 'How can a = a + 1? That's like saying 1 = 2. Mutable assignment makes no sense.'
The usual answer is 'because of C'. But that's just passing the buck: why does C do it that way?
Since assignment is about twice as frequent as equality testing in typical programs, it's appropriate that the operator be half as long.
CPL was a great language with a minor problem: nobody could implement it.
Looking at this as a whole, '=' was never 'the natural choice' the assignment operator.
We can trace C using it to CPL being such a trash fire.
COBOL is a bad language.
posts.key_insight: The use of '=' for assignment—now ubiquitous—was not a natural or obvious choice but rather an accident of history stemming from CPL's complexity, BCPL's simplifications, and Ken Thompson's preference for shorter code.

posts.summary: Hillel Wayne challenged the common assertion that functional programming is inherently easier to reason about than imperative programming by asking people to formally prove the correctness of three functions (Leftpad, Unique, and Fulcrum). The results showed that formal verification is difficult in both paradigms, with many FP advocates unable to complete proofs they initially dismissed as trivial. The formal verification community responded enthusiastically with solutions in numerous languages, while critics who claimed FP superiority without providing proofs had no overlap with those who actually completed the challenge. Wayne concludes that which paradigm is easier to reason about depends heavily on the specific problem domain rather than being a universal truth.
key_points: Formal correctness proofs are difficult regardless of paradigm - many who claimed FP proofs would be trivial failed to produce working solutions
There was zero overlap between people who aggressively criticized the challenge and those who actually submitted valid proofs
The heap and mutable state are what make imperative proofs harder, not the paradigm itself - Dafny proofs that avoided heap manipulation were essentially functional
Fulcrum was intentionally chosen to be harder functionally because proving properties about intermediate loop states is often easier with mutation than accumulators
The formal verification community was uniformly civil and engaged, while aggressive critics contributed nothing substantive
money_quotes: I don't like accepting things as axioms. If we make a claim, we better damn well put it to the test.
There was an immediate burst of people saying 'this will be easy' followed by the same people saying 'wait, this is actually pretty hard.'
What really surprised me was how there was zero overlap between the provers and the bulldogs.
None of the 'brilliant jerks' were willing to put any skin in the game. You don't have to listen if they have nothing to say.
At best you can say 'there are many more problems where it's easier to reason with FP than imperative', which I still think is probably wrong but at least isn't definitely wrong.
I chose correctness because it was the easiest to objectively verify.
Which paradigm is 'easier' to reason about heavily depends on your problem domain and algorithms you're writing.
posts.key_insight: Claims about programming paradigms being universally easier to reason about collapse under empirical testing - formal verification difficulty depends on the specific problem, not the paradigm.

posts.summary: Hillel Wayne shares an excerpt from Michael Jackson's book 'Software Requirements and Specifications' that challenges developers to honestly inventory the languages they use and test whether those languages can express common specification statements. The excerpt presents ten diverse requirements—from physical constraints on elevators to temporal conditions on vending machines—and asks readers to consider how they would formally express each. The implicit argument is that most developers lack adequate languages for expressing precise specifications, revealing a gap in our tooling for requirements engineering.
key_points: Most developers cannot easily express common specification statements in languages they actually use
Requirements span diverse domains: physical constraints, temporal logic, access control, event classification, and recursive definitions
The honest inventory of 'languages used for real work' is likely much smaller than languages developers claim to know
Specification languages serve a fundamentally different purpose than implementation languages
money_quotes: Be honest: don't list all of the languages you have ever heard of; include only languages you have used for real work at least once
The system is required to prevent two trains from occupying the same track segment at the same time.
If you lift the receiver when the phone is ringing that's an answer-call event; if it's not ringing it's a request-service event.
The vending machine can prevent a coin-insertion event, but only the customer can cause it.
Did you find it all easy? I didn't.
posts.key_insight: The languages developers actually use are often inadequate for expressing the precise specifications that real systems require.

posts.summary: Decision tables are a simple but underused formal specification technique for representing branching logic, where inputs map to exactly one output row. Wayne explains the concept in under two minutes, then demonstrates its practical value through a real-world migration of a legacy audio system with complex conditional logic involving text-to-speech, manual uploads, and database lookups. The table format made it easy to reason about edge cases like 'what happens if we have a legacy override pointing at a manual upload?' Decision tables can also reveal properties like input irrelevance and decomposition opportunities, and can be expanded into test suites.
key_points: A decision table maps boolean/enumeration inputs to outputs, with the consistency rule that all inputs must map to exactly one row
The technique excels at complex conditional logic with no clear pattern, where informal descriptions make reasoning difficult
Decision tables enable property discovery: determining which inputs are irrelevant and whether tables can be decomposed into simpler independent tables
They can be programmatically expanded into test suites to verify code consistency with the specification
The main advantage is accessibility: anyone can learn to read and write them with zero learning curve
money_quotes: I really like decision tables but they've fallen out of common knowledge. Let's fix that.
That's what I like about them: you can explain the whole idea in under two minutes.
Figuring out what should happen from the informal description is really hard. With a decision table, that question just maps to row 3.
Like all formal specifications, we can use the table to discover properties about our program.
Decision tables are an extremely lightweight formal specification.
Pretty much anyone can learn to write one. More importantly, anybody can learn to read one, so it provides a simple means of sharing information between people.
That's why it's a shame they're so obscure: a lot of people would find them useful and there's zero learning curve to start using them.
posts.key_insight: Decision tables are an accessible formal specification technique that makes complex conditional logic easy to reason about, verify, and communicate, yet they've become needlessly obscure.

posts.summary: Hillel Wayne demonstrates how formal methods can dramatically outperform traditional testing for finding concurrency bugs. He revisits the famous Extreme Programming Challenge Fourteen, where Agile practitioners struggled for weeks to find a bug in a bounded buffer implementation. Using TLA+, Wayne finds the same bug in just 31 minutes, compared to the 16+ hours it took the original challengers—and he argues the true comparison is even more favorable since the testers couldn't reliably detect if their fixes worked. The post concludes that formal methods should augment, not replace, Agile practices like testing and pairing.
key_points: The XP Challenge Fourteen exposed a fundamental limitation: testing concurrent code is exponentially harder because you must verify all possible execution orderings, not just one
TLA+ found the deadlock bug in 31 minutes by exhaustively checking all thread interleavings, while unit testing took 16 hours and still couldn't reliably verify fixes
The bug occurs when notify() wakes the wrong type of thread—a getter waking another getter instead of a putter—leading to deadlock when all putters are asleep
The model checker revealed the minimum thread count needed to trigger the bug (2*BuffLen + 1) and verified that notifyAll() fixes the issue
Formal methods have real limitations: specs aren't code, they can diverge from implementation, and checking large state spaces is slow—but these don't negate their value for design verification
money_quotes: Concurrency is evil. Synchronous algorithms have a single well-defined output for a given input. Concurrent algorithms have a set of possible outputs depending on the order of events.
There are n! ways to order n unique, independent events.

The difference between writing TLA+ and just writing unit tests isn't half an hour versus sixteen hours, it's half an hour versus 'Two weeks to realize there's a bug, a week to find the bug, three days to understand the bug, sixteen hours to write the test, twenty minutes to run the test, and you don't know if your fix really works.'
Before Cargill gave the solution, Wells claimed his unit tests showed there wasn't a bug. It's incredibly likely this would have gone into production.
If you write it without a spec, though, you don't know whether the code is buggy because the implementation is wrong or because the fundamental design is wrong.
Specifications give us a means of thinking quickly and deeply about complex systems and finding flaws in our designs.
posts.key_insight: Formal methods don't replace testing and other Agile practices—they augment them by catching design-level bugs in concurrent systems that testing fundamentally cannot find efficiently.

posts.summary: This tutorial introduces MiniZinc, a constraint solving language for modeling optimization problems, using a conference scheduling problem as the main example. The post walks through defining enums, parameters, decision variables, and constraints to create a schedule that maximizes attendee satisfaction. Wayne demonstrates how constraint languages make it easy to add new requirements—like ensuring speakers don't present twice on the same day—without rewriting the core algorithm. However, he notes that while expressing constraints is straightforward, optimizing models for real-world scale is challenging and poorly documented, which sets up a follow-up article on optimization techniques.
key_points: Constraint solvers are powerful but surprisingly obscure tools that many developers don't know exist
MiniZinc separates problem definition from solver implementation, allowing you to choose the best solver for your specific problem
Adding new constraints to a model is simple—you just add another constraint rather than rewriting the algorithm
The naive model works fast on small problems but fails to scale; 24 talks and 20 people took over 8 hours without optimization
Optimizing constraint models is highly heuristic-based and poorly documented, requiring significant trial and error
money_quotes: Constraint solvers are one of those things that are way more obscure than they should be.
A lot of people don't even know constraint solvers exist, much less how useful they are.
If we were writing the scheduling algorithm ourself, this would be a huge mess.
One nice thing about MiniZinc is it decouples the definition of the problem from the implementation.
While expressing constraints is easy, optimizing them is a topic of its own.
Three optimizations may be ten times faster than any two alone.
posts.key_insight: Constraint solvers are underappreciated tools that let you express complex optimization problems declaratively, though making them scale requires deep expertise in heuristic optimization.

posts.summary: This tutorial walks through optimizing a MiniZinc conference scheduling model, demonstrating how to reduce runtime from over 8 hours to just 2 seconds through various techniques. Wayne explores solver selection, constraint reformulation, symmetry breaking, and channeling while discovering that optimization is highly nonlinear—what helps with one solver or dataset may hurt with another. The key breakthrough comes from using channels to decompose the schedule into separate talk_time and talk_room arrays, combined with precomputing favorites and using native functions like nvalue instead of hand-rolled constraints.
key_points: Switching solvers can dramatically affect performance, but the 'best' solver depends on your specific constraints and optimizations
Symmetry breaking reduced runtime by an order of magnitude by eliminating equivalent solutions (from 5s to 0.5s)
Channels—constraints relating two representations of the same model—enabled the biggest improvement by making certain lookups more efficient
Native MiniZinc functions consistently outperform hand-rolled equivalents, even when the logic appears identical
Optimization improvements are nonlinear: a change that helps with one solver, dataset, or set of constraints may be a regression with another
money_quotes: Optimizations are nonlinear. Whether something's an improvement or regression depends on your other optimizations.
One solver's improvement is another solver's regression.
This could be the difference between 'finished in 300ms' and 'still going a year later'.
Native beats bespoke.
Casting arrays to sets is the road to madness.
I feel vaguely betrayed.
Profile everything and then don't trust it.
Even though our `fav` stepping-stone was a regression, the end result is five times faster than without the regression!
posts.key_insight: Constraint programming optimization is deeply nonlinear—improvements depend on solver choice, other constraints, and dataset, so systematic experimentation matters more than following rules.

posts.summary: Hillel Wayne announces the release of his book 'Practical TLA+', a comprehensive introduction to the formal specification language TLA+. He explains that TLA+ allows developers to write blueprints of systems and find complex bugs in minutes rather than days or weeks. The book is structured in two parts: the first covers syntax and semantics, while the second focuses on practical application techniques.
