Wayne emphasizes that the book is designed to be accessible, with extensive editing to ensure clarity at every step.
key_points: TLA+ is a formal specification language for writing system blueprints that can find complex bugs quickly
The language excels at handling concurrency problems, from threads to distributed databases
The book aims to take readers from complete outsiders to practical users in their day-to-day work
Part two focuses on real-world application: techniques, approaches, and pitfalls for writing specs
The author and technical editor spent an average of 30 minutes per page ensuring clarity
money_quotes: By writing and testing the specification you can find complex bugs in your system in minutes as opposed to days or weeks without it.
TLA+ is especially good for dealing with concurrency, easily handling everything from threads to globally distributed databases.
It's one of the most revolutionary tools I've ever encountered, but people assume something so powerful must be hard to learn and use.
I mean to change that.
Practical TLA+ is designed to take you from a complete outsider in formal methods to a happy user at your day-to-day job.
posts.key_insight: Powerful formal verification tools like TLA+ don't have to be hard to learn—the barrier is pedagogical, not technical.

posts.summary: This post demonstrates how to model message queues and pub-sub systems in TLA+, providing practical examples that go beyond the typical consensus algorithm tutorials. Hillel walks through implementing an SQS-like queue with writers and readers, then extends it to handle at-least-once delivery semantics with message duplication and retry logic. He then shows a pub-sub variant where each reader has their own queue, including subscription management. The post emphasizes that TLA+ is useful for everyday distributed systems problems, not just verifying Paxos, and calls for more accessible learning resources.
key_points: TLA+ lacks simple, practical examples which makes it harder to learn and harder for people to see its relevance to their actual problems
At-least-once delivery can be modeled three different ways depending on whether you're specifying the protocol itself or a system using the protocol
Type invariants in TLA+ serve double duty as both spec correctness checks and model constraint validation, which should ideally be separated
Function sets and function comprehensions are powerful TLA+ features that elegantly model per-reader queues in pub-sub systems
The either/or construct naturally models nondeterministic failures like dropped messages, failed acks, or message duplication
money_quotes: My problem isn't verifying Paxos, it's figuring out why my microservices aren't deduping the message queue.
If you looked at the most common public examples, you'd probably come away thinking that people only used it for critical consensus algorithms.
TLA+ is an untyped formalism. Lamport argued that type systems in specifications are often a hindrance.
I could just as easily make types like 'two-digit primes except 11' or 'sequences of messages no reader has seen yet.'
Mixing spec invariants and model constraints is bad practice. You should really separate them out.
Function sets are one of the biggest stumbling blocks for people new to TLA+, but they are amazingly powerful once you get used to them.
It took me only a couple minutes to whip each example up but I've been doing this a while- it's probably a lot harder to 'see' these if you're just starting out.
posts.key_insight: TLA+ is most valuable not for verifying exotic consensus algorithms but for modeling the everyday distributed systems problems engineers actually face, like message queue semantics and deduplication logic.

posts.summary: Hillel Wayne proposes replacing traditional technical interviews with a code review exercise where candidates study a codebase, then write comments on a pull request. This approach addresses nine specific problems with conventional interviews: time investment, stress, objectivity, granularity, bias, inaccuracy, scope, communication, and calibration. The PR review can be graded against a rubric, done double-blind to reduce bias, and produces artifacts useful for data analysis. Wayne acknowledges weaknesses including setup costs, requiring candidate prep time, and not testing code-writing ability directly.
key_points: Traditional interviews fail because whiteboard problems don't reflect actual programming work and subjective judgments introduce bias
A PR review exercise tests real skills: reading code, discussing changes, and communicating respectfully about someone's work
Adding 'personas' to PRs (junior developer vs senior lead) tests whether candidates adapt their communication style appropriately
Double-blind grading against a rubric provides objectivity and creates auditable records for calibration
The approach has genuine weaknesses: setup costs, language specificity, and losing the ability to follow up on candidate reasoning
money_quotes: Being able to write quicksort by heart does not show you know how to debug or optimize complex code.
Seeing how a candidate performs under interview stress does not tell you how they perform under 'someone deleted the production database' stress.
Reading code, discussing PRs, and evaluating changes to a system are all core parts of a programmer's job.
The tone and content of their comments should reflect these personas.
Since the candidate is only judged on the final artifact, we have a record of the PR, comments, rubric, and performance of the candidate.
posts.key_insight: Technical interviews should test the skills programmers actually use daily—reading code and communicating about changes—rather than artificial whiteboard exercises that don't reflect real work.

posts.summary: The post applies Nancy Leveson's STAMP (System-Theoretic Accident Model & Processes) framework to analyze the event-stream npm package compromise, where a malicious actor gained maintainer access and injected code to steal Bitcoin wallets. Rather than seeking a single 'root cause' to blame, STAMP reveals how the accident emerged from systemic properties: npm's ecosystem of thousands of small packages maintained by individuals who never expected responsibility, transitive dependencies that obscure accountability, lack of privilege restrictions in JavaScript, and inadequate tooling for auditing. The analysis identifies 15+ system-level factors that made the attack possible, arguing that fixing any single 'root cause' won't prevent similar attacks without addressing the deeper structural issues.
key_points: Root cause analysis provides 'an illusion of control' - accidents are symptomatic of deeper system failures, not isolated mistakes by individuals
The npm ecosystem's culture of many small packages creates massive attack surfaces with ~1700 maintainers for a single app's dependencies
JavaScript lacks Principle of Least Privilege enforcement - any dependency can make HTTP requests or access the filesystem
Model drift occurs when practices designed for one context (browser JS needing minification) become security vulnerabilities in new contexts (Electron apps)
Floating dependencies create a conflict between two safety constraints: auditing changes slowly vs. applying security patches quickly
money_quotes: 'Who did this' is the wrong question. 'How did this happen' is the wrong question. A better question is 'why was this possible in the first place?'
Accidents aren't isolated failures. Accidents aren't human error. Accidents aren't simple. Accidents are complicated.
One law we see time and time again is 'you cannot fix things with discipline.'

Auditing is a waste of time. Most packages aren't going to be malicious. Copay had 2700 dependencies. After the 200th time auditing an update and going 'yup, checks out', are you really going to be as diligent with the 201st?

Tarr didn't do any advertising. He is not a famous person. He just happened to be using Node a little before everybody else, needed to write a utility, and decided to publicly release it. Suddenly it was critical to thousands of projects.
Fixing the 'root cause' is fast and cheap. Changing the fundamental system properties is slow, expensive, and risky.
posts.key_insight: Security incidents in software ecosystems cannot be prevented by assigning blame to individuals; they require examining and addressing the systemic properties—cultural, technical, and organizational—that made the failure possible in the first place.

posts.summary: This post provides a comprehensive historical overview of why formal methods remain niche in software engineering despite their potential benefits. Wayne distinguishes between code verification (proving code matches specifications) and design verification (proving system designs are correct), explaining that code verification is technically difficult—requiring extensive mathematical proofs that even with modern SMT solvers yield only about four lines of verified code per day. Design verification is technically easier but faces cultural barriers: programmers don't trust artifacts that aren't code and don't believe specification provides value beyond existing practices like pseudocode or TDD. The post argues that while full formal verification is rarely economical, partial verification and design specification offer practical value that most of the industry overlooks.
key_points: Code verification requires proving programs match specifications, which is extraordinarily difficult—Microsoft's IronFleet project produced only 4 lines of verified code per day despite using cutting-edge SMT solvers
The 'right spec' problem isn't primarily about validation (does the customer want this?) but about translating human concepts into mathematical terms we can formally express
Design verification is technically much easier than code verification because you can abstract away implementation details, but faces social/cultural adoption barriers
Most software doesn't need 100% correctness—you can get to 99% with testing, types, and practices like Cleanroom at far lower cost than formal proofs
Model checkers make design verification accessible by brute-forcing state spaces instead of requiring proofs, though they face state-space explosion challenges
money_quotes: There really isn't a formal methods community so much as a few tiny bands foraging in the Steppe.
Proofs are hard. Obnoxiously hard. 'Quit programming and join the circus' hard.

By using advanced SMT solvers and a cutting-edge verification language, Microsoft was able to write 5,000 lines of verified Dafny code in only 3.7 person-years! That's a blazing-fast rate of four whole lines a day.
What's good for coding is bad for proving!
You do not need full code verification to write good software or even to write near-perfect software.
While code verification is a technical problem, design verification is a social problem: people just don't see the point.
If there's one thing that studying formal methods has taught me, it's that time is evil and I hate it.
Keep in mind 'a lot of specifiers' is, like, ten people.
posts.key_insight: Formal methods remain niche not because they don't work, but because code verification is prohibitively expensive for most cases while design verification—though technically easier—faces cultural resistance from programmers who don't trust non-code artifacts.

posts.summary: This post demonstrates how to use Alloy, a formal modeling tool, to systematically solve Knights and Knaves logic puzzles rather than relying on manual reasoning. Wayne walks through the Alloy syntax for modeling these puzzles, where Knights always tell the truth and Knaves always lie, showing how the model-finder can discover all valid solutions. The post explores increasingly complex variations, including the famous fork-in-the-road puzzle and an advanced version with drunk/sober modifiers. Along the way, Wayne reveals counterintuitive results, such as how the famous fork puzzle solution only works in a special case, while a simpler general solution exists.
key_points: Alloy's model-finding capability can exhaustively discover all solutions to logic puzzles, making it superior to manual solving for verification
The logical equivalence operator (<=> ) elegantly captures the knight/knave truth-telling constraint: a statement is true iff the speaker is a knight
Propositional logic produces unintuitive results: 'I am a knave and B is a knave' differs logically from 'I am a knave. B is a knave.'
The famous fork-in-the-road solution only works when there's exactly one knight and one knave; the general case is actually simpler
Alloy's type system with 'extends' vs 'in' signatures allows modeling both exclusive categories (knight/knave) and overlapping properties (drunk/sober)
money_quotes: Knights and Knaves puzzles are supposed to be solved through creativity and cleverness: you read the statements and figure out the contradictions. Let's instead take all the fun out and solve them with a program.
This means, confusingly enough, that if we want to know whether P is true or false, we don't need to know whether we're talking to a knight or a knave.
Propositional logic is weird and often unintuitive.
The problem is that all we can infer from 'F is a knave' is 'E did not say there is one knight among us.' It does not restrict what E said, or what she could say later.
It's really funny to me that the famous solution only works in this special case, while the general case is both simpler and always gets true answers regardless of what pair you have.
Not only that, but the general case doesn't even use o! It works even if we're talking to a single person.
posts.key_insight: Formal modeling tools like Alloy can mechanically solve logic puzzles while also revealing subtle flaws in 'classic' solutions that human reasoning might miss.

posts.summary: This guide argues that formal specifications are practical tools for everyday software engineering, not just for building new systems from scratch. Wayne presents specifications as high-level, partial, and imperfect documents that exist apart from code and complement (rather than replace) testing. He outlines a progression of specification uses from documenting existing systems to building new ones, emphasizing that most benefit comes from clarifying understanding and catching design bugs early. The post also honestly addresses when specs aren't worthwhile: low-cost errors, implementation-level code, ambiguous requirements, and human-in-the-loop systems.
key_points: Specs are high-level, partial, and imperfect—they don't need to describe total system behavior and diminishing returns apply
Specifications catch design errors while tests catch implementation errors; you need both
The biggest value often comes before model checking—writing precise specs clarifies thinking and makes bugs 'obvious'
Specs exist apart from code with no technical debt risk, making them low-friction to experiment with
Most practical FM use is iterative and incremental, not exhaustive upfront specification
money_quotes: Tests are good for showing your code matches your expectations but very bad for showing your expectations match your needs. Specs are the opposite.
It's much easier to see a race condition in 10 lines of spec than in 1,000 lines of code.

This leads to a common beginner frustration with formal specifications, that they've already figured things out before finishing the model. This is actually one of the big benefits of specification: most of the model checking can happen in your head.

The spec exists apart from your code and is not a dependency. This means that trying specifications, unlike trying new languages, does not carry a risk of technical debt or add any maintenance burden.
You can't really handwave in a spec: everything you write must be precise and rigorous. This does wonders for understanding something.
We're in the sweet spot where some form of specification helps a lot, but the vast majority of us don't use it.
The spec is there to amplify your ability to think through the design, not force you on a rigid path.
posts.key_insight: Formal specifications provide the most value not through exhaustive verification but by forcing precise thinking about designs, making subtle bugs obvious before they reach code.

posts.summary: Metamorphic testing is a powerful testing technique that compares outputs across transformed inputs rather than checking against known answers. Unlike traditional oracle-based tests or property-based testing, metamorphic testing leverages 'metamorphic relations' - properties that should hold between related inputs and their outputs. The technique is especially valuable for complex systems like speech-to-text where generating test oracles is expensive or impossible. Despite impressive academic case studies across many domains, the technique remains obscure because nearly all research is locked behind academic paywalls.
key_points: Traditional oracle tests don't scale for complex systems with enormous input spaces, and property-based testing often yields only weak general properties
Metamorphic testing compares outputs across transformed inputs (e.g., same audio at different volumes should produce same transcription) without needing to know the 'correct' answer
The technique has been successfully applied to search engines, machine learning systems, compilers, and scientific simulations with documented bug discoveries
Academic paywalls are the primary barrier to adoption - finding case studies requires hours of hunting for preprints
Metamorphic testing and property-based testing are complementary: PBT focuses on input generation while MT focuses on what properties to test
money_quotes: We have useful tests without listening to the voice clip. We can now generate complex, deep tests without the use of an oracle!
For complex systems, it can be easier to find interesting metamorphic relations than interesting single input/output properties.
When I dig into obscure techniques, I always ask 'why is it obscure?' Sometimes there's an obvious reason, sometimes it's a complex set of subtle reasons, sometimes it's just bad luck.
Almost all of the info is behind academic paywalls. If you want to learn about MT, you either need journal access or spend hours hunting down preprints.
Confession: I read the ACM Magazine. This makes me a dweeb even in programming circles.
In general it seems to me that most PBT research focuses on how we effectively generate and shrink inputs, while MT research is more focused on determining what we actually want to test.
posts.key_insight: Metamorphic testing unlocks practical testing of complex systems by checking that relationships between transformed inputs hold, rather than requiring expensive or impossible-to-obtain test oracles.

posts.summary: The post examines how property-based testing with contracts can catch subtle bugs that manual unit tests miss, using a buggy Python mode() function as a case study. The bug—treating 0 as falsy when checking for an uninitialized max value—only surfaces with specific input combinations unlikely to appear in hand-written tests. Wayne walks through progressively sophisticated contract approaches: type checks (unhelpful), sanity checks (partial), comparing against first element (catches bug), and testing the mathematical definition (most robust). He also explores metamorphic properties like sorting preservation and affine transformations, showing how different approaches trade off between power and overconstraint.
key_points: Property-based testing finds bugs in subtle input intersections that manual tests miss because humans don't think to test edge cases like 'mode is falsy AND last element differs'
Sanity check contracts (e.g., 'result is in list') are useful but insufficient—they don't express enough about the function's actual semantics to catch implementation bugs
The most robust contract tests the mathematical definition directly: the mode's count must be >= every other element's count
Oracle-based testing overconstrains by forcing a specific choice when multiple valid answers exist (e.g., ties in mode)
Static typing and dynamic contracts are complementary—each catches bugs the other misses, like None being a valid list element
money_quotes: To surface the bug, the mode of a list must be a falsy value like 0 or [] and the last value of the list must be something else. It's a small intersection of Python's typing and the mechanics of mode, making it too unusual a case to be found by standard unit testing practice.
The problem with PBT, though, is that it can be hard to find good properties.
We want a total contract, one which always raises on an incorrect output and never raises on a correct one.
With math_mode, we're arbitrarily choosing one of them as the 'real' mode and checking that our mode picked that arbitrary element.
Static and dynamic analysis are complementary, not contradictory.
Hypothetically contracts can express all possible properties of a function. In practice you're limited to what your framework can express and check.
posts.key_insight: The best property tests encode the mathematical definition of correctness rather than implementation-specific checks, but finding that definition requires understanding what makes your function unique among similar operations.

posts.summary: This post demonstrates how to model adversaries and environmental effects in TLA+ specifications. Wayne shows how to compose a 'machine' (the system we control) with a 'world' (outside forces we can't control) and explores what properties can still be guaranteed. The key insight is that while stability (staying in goal forever) may be impossible with adversaries, we can still prove weaker but useful properties like resilience (eventually returning to goal) or finite-spite stability. The post also shows how to verify that the machine itself never violates safety invariants, even if the world can.
key_points: Separate your spec into 'machine' (controllable) and 'world' (uncontrollable) components to reason about adversarial or environmental effects
When full stability is impossible, weaken requirements to prove useful properties like resilience (always-eventually safe) or finite-world stability
Verify that your machine maintains invariants even when the world can violate them - this confirms the controllable parts behave correctly
Model adversaries as agents with a superset of normal actions - they can follow the protocol OR break it, making the adversarial case the general one
The update correction shows a subtle pitfall: when machine actions are a subset of world actions, fairness on the machine makes finite-world assumptions vacuously true
money_quotes: These 'open world' situations are a great place to use formal methods. We can't easily represent rock-dropping with line-of-code verification.
Any restrictions to the world is a weakening of our spec.
A system is stable if it can't be pushed out of Goal. A system is resilient if, after being pushed out of Goal, it eventually returns to Goal.
The world is free to violate our invariants, but our machine isn't. That's what we can control, and that's what we want to confirm does nothing unsafe.
We can think of an adversary as an agent in the system who can take a superset of the actions everybody else can.
This means that the general case of our spec is the one where everybody is an attacker, and the 'normal' case is actually the exceptional one!
Everybody comes up with their own meanings for 'stability', 'robustness', and 'resilience' and they all contradict each other.
posts.key_insight: When modeling adversaries or environmental effects, separate your specification into controllable 'machine' and uncontrollable 'world' components, then explore what weaker-but-still-useful properties you can guarantee when full stability is impossible.

posts.summary: This post debunks the persistent myth that Alan Kay invented objects, tracing the concept back to Simula, which the Smalltalk team explicitly acknowledged as inspiration. Wayne examines Kay's own contradictory statements over the years and argues that the modern revisionist view that OOP is 'really about messages, not objects' doesn't match the historical record. The post emphasizes that Smalltalk's genuine innovations—like everything being an object and the doesNotUnderstand mechanism—are distinct from inventing the core concept. Wayne concludes that different OOP pioneers solved different problems, and our modern understanding is a synthesis of many contributors' ideas.
key_points: Objects came from Simula, which the Smalltalk-72 manual and 1981 Byte magazine explicitly cite as the major inspiration
Kay's 1998 claim to have invented objects contradicts earlier primary sources and he later stopped making this claim
The revisionist view that OOP was always 'about messages, not objects' doesn't match early Smalltalk documentation, which treated classes, objects, and messages as three co-equal pillars
Smalltalk's genuine innovations include making everything an object (including classes and messages) and the doesNotUnderstand mechanism that decouples messaging from object internals
Kay and the Simula creators were solving different problems (personal computing vs simulation), so asking who used objects 'right' is nonsensical
money_quotes: Interviews done 30 years later are not good primary sources.
People don't invent tools in a vacuum. They have specific situations and problems and try to find solutions to those problems.
So who used objects 'right'? That's not a sensible question. They did different things because they had different problems.

Our modern idea of OOP is a synthesis of all their ideas, as well as the ideas of Adele Goldberg, Barbara Liskov, David Parnas, Bertrand Meyer, Gul Agha, and many others. But none of them can lay claim to what OOP 'really means'.
Terms evolve as problems do.
This arguably makes Powershell the spiritual successor to Smalltalk.
posts.key_insight: The history of OOP is a synthesis of many contributors solving different problems, and retroactive claims about what it 'really means' don't hold up against primary sources.

posts.summary: This post catalogs intermediate-level Vim tricks that fall between beginner tutorials and expert deep-dives. Wayne argues that Vim's power comes not from modal editing but from its density of special-purpose tools crammed into every available keystroke. He divides Vim users into 'purists' who keep minimal configuration and 'exobrains' who customize extensively. The article covers visual mode operators, Ex commands like :g and :norm, statusline customization, autocommands, and useful plugins, all presented as discrete tricks rather than a cohesive system.
key_points: Vim's core value proposition is density - having 'show all matching tags' be just g] makes you actually use it
The gap between beginner and expert Vim content leaves intermediate users to discover tricks accidentally for years
Visual mode operators (v, V, ctrl-V) can modify how motions behave, converting linewise to blockwise deletions
The :g/regex/norm pattern combines global matching with normal mode commands, often easier than macros
Vim stores a full undo tree rather than linear history, letting you recover any previous state
money_quotes: I've been using Vim for eight years and am still discovering new things. This is usually seen as a Good Thing About Vim. In my head, though, it's a failing of discoverability.
Vim is a patchwork of subsystems crammed together with every crag of space stuffed with extra special-purpose tooling.
When 'show all matching tags for keyword' is just g] then you're much more likely to actually use it.
But nobody really talks about those special purpose tricks, leading people to stumble into stuff they needed for the past six years.
Exobrains stuff Vim full of plugins, functions, and homebrew mappings in a vain attempt to pretend they're using Emacs.
A lot of Vim's prime real-estate is taken up by cruft. s saves one whole keystroke over doing just cl.
That's moving beyond the realm of 'Vim tricks' and into the realm of 'dark magic'.
posts.key_insight: Vim's power lies not in modal editing philosophy but in the sheer density of special-purpose commands packed into available keystrokes, though this same density creates discoverability problems that leave users stumbling onto essential features years too late.

posts.summary: Hillel Wayne explores PRISM, a probabilistic modeling language that can express statistical properties like average times and probability distributions—capabilities that TLA+ and Alloy lack. Using a game theory example of two players with different skill levels, he demonstrates how PRISM can calculate win probabilities and optimal handicaps. However, he concludes that PRISM's severe expressivity limitations—no tuples, arrays, functions, or even strings—make it impractical for most real-world problems despite its powerful probability checking capabilities.
key_points: PRISM fills a gap that TLA+ and Alloy cannot: expressing statistical properties and weighted probabilities rather than absolute correctness
The fundamental tradeoff in modeling is that more powerful checkers require less expressive languages—PRISM's probability analysis comes at the cost of having only integers, doubles, and booleans
PRISM uses an extended Linear Temporal Logic (LTL) to express properties with operators like X (next), F (finally/eventually), G (global/henceforth), and S (steady-state)
The experiment feature allows running properties over parameter ranges to generate tables and graphs automatically
Despite months of searching, the author struggled to find good examples because most interesting problems require strings, tuples, or functions that PRISM lacks
money_quotes: There's a fundamental tradeoff in modeling: the more powerful the checker, the less expressive the language.
TLA+ and Alloy are good for absolutes. You can say 'there exists an error in this design'. But you can't express statistical properties.
As far as I can tell, this is a conceptual tradeoff: part of the reason PRISM is able to even check complex probabilities is because the spec language is so limited.
It took me months to find a good example because everything I could think of either required strings, tuples, or functions.
There are lots of interesting problems that you might want to solve, but very, very few of them are cleanly expressible in PRISM.
Is it worth learning? I don't think so. It's not going to be useful unless you're absolutely sure you don't need tuples.
But that's part of the deal with exploring: not everything you find is gonna shake the world.
posts.key_insight: PRISM demonstrates the fundamental tension in formal tools between analytical power and practical expressivity—its ability to check complex probabilities comes at the cost of a language so limited that few real problems can be cleanly modeled in it.

posts.summary: The author reflects on his EMT training experience where he discovered that electronic patient care reports (ePCRs), despite being objectively better than paper in almost every way, went unused because of a quarter-second lag on interactions. This seemingly trivial performance issue meant EMTs stuck with error-prone paper forms. At the scale of 30 million PCRs filed annually in the US, even small inefficiencies or error rates translate into lost doctor-hours and potentially lost lives. The post argues that performance isn't just a technical concern but a moral one when software operates at scale.
key_points: A quarter-second lag was enough to make EMTs abandon a superior electronic system entirely and return to error-prone paper forms
At scale, small probabilities become certainties: 0.1% of 30 million PCRs with errors means 30,000 wasted doctor-hours
Software doesn't need to be 'critical' in the traditional sense to have life-or-death consequences at sufficient scale
The diffuse, statistical nature of these harms makes them invisible and difficult to reason about
Performance decisions made during development may have consequences the developers never anticipated or considered
money_quotes: At that scale, low chances kill people. Murdered by statistics.
At that scale, time and life are interchangeable.
It's a factor so grand and diffuse we can't see it in motion. We can't think in those terms.
Paper was slow and annoying and easy to screw up, but at least it wasn't *that*.
Did they say 'premature optimization is bad' and not think about performance until it was too late?
Most of us aren't writing critical software. But this isn't 'critical software', either: nobody will suddenly die if it breaks.
Can a slow CSV library eventually cause a car accident? I don't know.
posts.key_insight: Performance isn't a luxury or premature optimization—at sufficient scale, a quarter-second lag can be the difference between software that saves lives and software that sits unused while people die from preventable errors.

posts.summary: The post demonstrates how to use Alloy, a formal specification language, to model and verify database migrations. Wayne walks through specifying an old schema (Person with event relationships) and a new schema (separate Attendance and Review tables), then proves that migrations preserve data equivalence and validity constraints. The tutorial reveals subtle bugs in the migration spec—like accidentally allowing extra records—and shows how formal methods catch these issues. The post concludes by refining the validity assertions to match real-world requirements: migrations should preserve validity, and the new schema should only allow states that would have been valid in the old schema.
key_points: Alloy's relational model elegantly expresses database schemas and the relationships between them, with relations being first-class entities that can be manipulated
The migration verification process has four steps: specify both schemas, define equivalence, prove equivalent data has equivalent constraints, and prove migrations produce equivalent data
Formal specification caught a subtle bug: the initial migration spec allowed creating extra records beyond what the original data contained
When the new schema has stronger constraints than the old one, you must split validity preservation into two properties: migrations stay valid, and new validity implies old validity
Alloy's grammar fits in two pages yet can express complex properties like multistage migrations and rollbacks
money_quotes: Migrations are risky business because they involve changing a large amount of state at once.
In specifications, we don't restrict ourselves to a single model, and instead talk about the entire space of possibilities.
If we can express a property of the spec, we automatically have the complete test for it.
We've made the NewPerson constraints stronger without changing the corresponding Person constraints.
Changing equivalent would mix our abstraction levels.
Alloy packs a lot of power in a very small space.
posts.key_insight: Formal methods aren't just for distributed systems—they're equally valuable for verifying database migrations by proving that data transformations preserve both equivalence and validity constraints.

posts.summary: Hillel Wayne investigates his speaking pace at conference talks, which he knows is too fast. He uses AWS transcription to analyze two of his talks, discovering he speaks at about 194 words per minute—significantly faster than Obama's 120 wpm baseline. Through practice with specific pacing exercises and a custom vim configuration to estimate talk lengths, he successfully reduced his pace to 163 wpm for a subsequent talk while covering the same amount of content, proving that slowing down doesn't sacrifice information density.
key_points: AWS transcription, despite being inaccurate, is sufficient for word-count analysis since perfect accuracy isn't needed for calculating speaking pace
His natural conference speaking rate of ~194 wpm with peaks of 221 wpm is significantly faster than the 120 wpm baseline of measured speakers like Obama
Practicing with 2-minute chunks at target pace (130 wpm) proved more effective than shorter samples because speech naturally varies in tempo
J programming language, despite its terseness, wasn't the ideal data querying tool he hoped—he spent 3 hours golfing code that provided no better results than his 10-minute version
Slowing down to 163 wpm covered the same ~6000 words of content in 6 more minutes, proving reduced pace doesn't mean reduced information
money_quotes: It's like the words are piled up in my mouth and I can't say one without the rest tumbling out.
I don't need *good* transcription, just *any* transcription.
That's a peak speed of over 220 words per minute. I need to slow down.

I need to emphasize that this entire process was *pointless*. The 3 hour version didn't get me better data than the version I wrote in ten minutes. That pokes a hole in my 'J as a query language' idea.
That's the big problem with J: it gets you obsessed with golfing.
It's feasible to introspect the way I do things and then use that information to do them better.
Slowing down is easier on both me and the audience, but it doesn't come at the cost of information.
posts.key_insight: Automated transcription, even when inaccurate, provides sufficient data for self-analysis, and deliberate practice with measurable targets can materially improve professional skills like public speaking.

posts.summary: Hyperproperties are properties defined over sets of behaviors rather than individual traces, making them fundamentally different from traditional safety and liveness properties. While TLA+ cannot natively express hyperproperties, you can work around this limitation through 'self-composition'—creating a hyperspec that runs multiple copies of the original spec and compares their results. The post walks through a concrete example of checking Observational Determinism (whether a nondeterministic system appears deterministic to outside observers) using this technique. Most hyperproperty research focuses on security properties like noninterference, though the formalism has seen limited industrial adoption. Wayne argues that understanding hyperproperties provides valuable intuition for verification, explaining why tests are better at capturing relationships between function calls than types or contracts.
key_points: Hyperproperties are properties defined over sets of behaviors, not individual traces—'average response time under 10ms' can't be refuted by a single counterexample
TLA+ fundamentally cannot express hyperproperties natively, but self-composition (running multiple spec copies in parallel) converts k-safety hyperproperties into regular 1-safety properties
Observational Determinism—where a system appears deterministic to observers despite internal nondeterminism—is a 2-safety hyperproperty requiring two traces as a counterexample
Security properties like noninterference are hyperproperties, which partly explains why security is so hard to get right
Tests are better than types/contracts at capturing hyperproperties because they can run functions multiple times, similar to how hyperspecs run specs multiple times
money_quotes: Properties which are only defined for sets of behaviors are hyperproperties.
You can't provide a single timeline as a counterexample; for all we know, all the other timelines get the same x value.
Specifying hyperproperties in TLA+: You can't. This is a fundamental limit of the language.
That's the challenge with emulation: sure, you can sort of hack in what you need, but maybe you're using the wrong tool for the job.
Many interesting security properties are hyperproperties, which is one reason why they're so hard to get right.
While you're not going to be formally verifying hyperproperties any time soon, I still think they're very useful. It helps you think about systems in a different way.
'Was this a correct optimization' and 'Was this a safe refactoring' are hyperproperties.
posts.key_insight: Understanding hyperproperties—properties over sets of behaviors rather than individual traces—explains why security is hard, why tests catch things types miss, and provides a powerful lens for thinking about system correctness.

posts.summary: Hillel Wayne presents a practical business case for formal methods, arguing that specification-based design verification saves money by catching complex bugs before implementation. He demonstrates using a carbon credit trading platform example where a subtle race condition—taking three actors and four steps to manifest—would slip past unit tests and QA. A 50-line TLA+ specification finds this bug in under six minutes total effort. He also honestly addresses limitations: no automatic code generation, less useful for simple systems, and poor at catching implementation errors like null handling.
key_points: Formal methods are 'debuggable design'—you can test system designs without writing code, finding bugs before implementation begins
Complex bugs are expensive: they're subtle enough to evade testing, dangerous enough to violate core requirements, and costly to fix after deployment
The carbon credit demo shows a race condition that unit tests won't find: offering the same credit to multiple parties allows ownership to transfer from someone who never offered it
Specifications aren't silver bullets—they don't auto-generate code, aren't worth it for simple systems, and won't catch implementation bugs like null handling
An hour of modeling catches issues that days of writing tests will miss, because it operates at a higher level of abstraction
money_quotes: You write a specification of your system and properties you want it to have. Then you can directly test the design without having to write any code and see if it has problems.
An hour of modeling will catch issues that days of writing tests will miss.
It took the model checker two seconds to find the error. In less than six minutes I designed the system and found an expensive bug.
So subtle, complex, dangerous. These are expensive bugs. The longer this bug is in production, the more money you lose.
If you can keep the whole system 'in your head', or if it does not involve a lot of intricate logic, specifications may not provide you much benefit.
Formal methods can be the difference between 'on time and under budget' and 'two months late and then another two months of maintenance fires.'
posts.key_insight: Formal methods pay for themselves by finding subtle, expensive bugs at the design stage—before you've spent weeks building the wrong system.

posts.summary: Feature interaction bugs occur when individually correct features combine to break global system properties. Wayne demonstrates this through a detailed example of an email registration system where spam blocking, email changing, and verification resending features each work correctly in isolation but combine to allow spammers through. These bugs are especially insidious because they don't map to isolated components and can't be caught by unit tests. While formal specification tools like TLA+ and Alloy can detect these issues, ultimately addressing feature interaction requires involving domain experts, maintaining constant communication, and continuously evaluating how local changes affect the big picture.
key_points: Global correctness does not follow from local correctness - individually rock-solid parts can combine into a broken system
Feature interaction bugs are undetectable by unit tests because they're global properties that emerge from combinations of features
Agile teams are especially vulnerable to FI bugs because iterative development creates space for cross-sprint interactions that weren't considered together
Formal specifications can test for FI at the global level, unlike code-level testing which is inherently local
FI is ultimately a technical problem with a social solution - requiring domain experts, communication, and big-picture thinking
money_quotes: In practice, global correctness does not follow from local correctness.
No part of our system is obviously broken. All of our unit tests pass. It's only when the different parts combine does the bug emerge that's not localizable to just one part failing.
Feature interactions have been a notorious source of runaway complexity, software bugs, cost and schedule overruns, and unfortunate user experiences.

Safety and security concerns are especially vulnerable to FI, as there's often no obviously-incorrect prior state. The system looks like it's working fine up until the point that everything catches fire.
Customers 'think locally' about changes to the system. They're not thinking about how feature X would interact with something you implemented six weeks ago.
The price for working at the machine level is that we are restricted to what we can express at the machine level.

Ultimately, dealing with FI bugs is a process of looping domain experts into your development process, keeping everyone in constant communication, and constantly reevaluating how local changes affect the big picture. It's a technical problem with a social solution.
posts.key_insight: Feature interaction bugs emerge when individually correct features combine to violate global properties, making them invisible to unit tests and requiring formal specification or domain expert involvement to detect.

posts.summary: Hillel Wayne traces the dramatic saga of a 2014 academic paper claiming to link programming language choice to code defect rates through GitHub mining. The original FSE paper made significant methodological errors, including misclassifying C++ projects as TypeScript and counting duplicate commits from Bitcoin forks. A 2019 replication study exposed these flaws, finding that one-third of 'defect-fixing commits' weren't actually bug fixes. The ensuing academic feud, complete with rebuttals and counter-rebuttals, reveals how science functions as a social institution where trust, integrity, and reputation matter as much as data. Wayne ultimately sides with the replicators after discovering the original authors failed to properly read a spreadsheet that refuted their own rebuttal.
key_points: The original study's most damning flaw was that approximately 36% of commits classified as 'bug fixes' weren't actually bug fixes at all
Replication is essential but rarely happens because there's little academic prestige in it, even when contradicting flawed influential papers
The original authors made repeated careless errors: classifying Bitcoin as TypeScript, counting duplicate commits across 17 Bitcoin forks, and miscalculating logarithms
Open data sharing is crucial for scientific integrity - the original paper's errors stayed hidden for years because the dataset wasn't publicly available
The rebuttal's strongest argument against the replication collapsed because the authors didn't scroll right on a GitHub CSV to see the third column
money_quotes: Don't trust anything that's not replicated.
For all we focus on 'objectivity' and 'evidence', it takes place in human institutions and relies on how humans work. We care about integrity, trustworthiness, and reputation.
If the code is not provided, then the paper is wrong, end of discussion.
Science is a social process. It's not enough to be right, you also have to be convincing. Regardless of how good your work is, if you sound like a crank nobody will believe you.
It's the same kind of error that FSE made several times: some data looked good for them, so they didn't investigate if it was actually good data.
We like to think of science as unbiased and purely rational. All we're supposed to care about is the facts and the data. But that's not the whole story.
Science has its problems, but it's still the best we got.
posts.key_insight: Science functions as a social institution where markers of trust and integrity serve as necessary heuristics, making replication and open data essential not just for accuracy but for the credibility that allows valid critiques to be heard.

posts.summary: This post surveys ten historically significant programming languages that are now mostly forgotten or dead, arguing that their influence on modern computing far exceeds their current recognition. Wayne methodically traces how features we take for granted—from structured data to OOP to type inference—originated in languages like COBOL, ALGOL, SIMULA, and CLU. He emphasizes that determining true influence requires citation chains, not just temporal precedence, since independent invention is common. The post serves as both a history lesson and a critique of lists that focus only on currently popular languages while ignoring the foundational work that made them possible.
key_points: True historical influence must be traced through citations and explicit acknowledgments, not just temporal precedence—being first doesn't mean you actually influenced successors
ALGOL is the most important of the four 'mother languages' despite being the least remembered today, with virtually every modern language inheriting its features
CLU might be the most influential language nobody has heard of, introducing iterators, abstract data types, generics, and checked exceptions
Many influential languages died not from technical flaws but from social factors: academia's disdain for COBOL, Smalltalk's collision with Java's marketing juggernaut, Pascal's unclear decline
The Encyclopedia of Programming Languages lists over 8,000 languages, suggesting countless innovations have been lost because their ideas never escaped their bubble
money_quotes: Just knowing that X was the first language with feature Z doesn't mean that X actually influenced Z.

Of the four mother languages, ALGOL is the most 'dead'; Everybody still knows about LISP, COBOL still powers tons of legacy systems, and most scientific packages still have some FORTRAN. But I've met plenty of programmers who haven't even heard of ALGOL.
ALGOL's children buried it.
CLU might be the most influential language that nobody's ever heard of. Iterators? CLU. Abstract data types? CLU. Generics? CLU. Checked exceptions? CLU.
Liskov wanted to get people to adopt her ideas, not her specific language. And they did.
The interesting question isn't 'Why did Smalltalk die', it's 'Why did C++ survive'.

Most influential languages never went mainstream. Few people used any one of them. But each one inspired people, who inspired other people, so the DNA of these forgotten languages appear decades after they're forgotten.
That's one reason I love studying history. To learn what we've lost and find it again.
posts.key_insight: The most influential programming languages in history are often not the most popular ones, but forgotten research languages whose ideas were absorbed by their successors—making the study of 'dead' languages essential for understanding modern computing.

posts.summary: Hillel Wayne announces the first draft of an online Alloy reference documentation hosted on ReadTheDocs. He argues that Alloy's biggest adoption barrier isn't technical but social—requiring people to buy a book to learn it. The documentation covers language syntax, tooling, techniques for better specs, and troubleshooting. While still incomplete and unofficial, Wayne believes it will help people learn Alloy and welcomes community feedback via GitHub.
key_points: Alloy's entire syntax fits in two pages and can be learned in a day
The biggest barrier to Alloy adoption is social, not technological—requiring a book purchase to learn
The new online documentation covers syntax, tooling, techniques, and common issues
The documentation is a first draft seeking community feedback before becoming official
money_quotes: It's a simple, powerful formal method whose entire syntax fits in just two pages.
You can learn it in a day and be productive in two.
The biggest barrier to Alloy adoption wasn't technological but social: in order to learn it, you have to buy the book.
While the book is excellent, that's also incredibly inconvenient for most people.
There needs to be a convenient and free way to learn Alloy.
posts.key_insight: Technical tools often fail to gain adoption not because of their complexity, but because of accessibility barriers like requiring paid resources to learn them.

posts.summary: Wayne captures the frustrating experience of trying to adopt a new programming language despite theoretical advantages. While online tutorials make languages look appealing, the practical obstacles of tooling, documentation, debugging, and ecosystem are overwhelming. The accumulating friction of answering dozens of mundane questions—none addressed by tutorials—drives developers back to familiar languages. The post is a compact catalog of the real barriers to language adoption that have nothing to do with syntax or semantics.
key_points: Language tutorials teach syntax but ignore the practical knowledge needed to actually build things
The tooling ecosystem (editors, debuggers, package managers) is often the real barrier to adoption
Documentation organization and community support quality matter as much as language features
Platform-specific installation issues immediately undermine cross-platform language claims
The cumulative weight of small unknowns overwhelms the theoretical benefits of switching languages
money_quotes: I want to do $THING. Normally I do my hacking in Python, which is *okay* but has lots of frustrations.
How do I install it? The docs say `brew install`, but I'm on Windows.
Is this ostensibly editor-agnostic but *really* supposed to use Emacs?
What are the language quirks that will cost me an hour to discover?
Will doing anything other than "break into repl" be like pulling teeth?
So… the language community.
Screw this, I'm going back to Python.
posts.key_insight: The hard part of learning a new language isn't the language itself—it's the hundred practical questions about tooling, ecosystem, and workflow that no tutorial ever answers.

posts.summary: Hillel Wayne explains why he prefers Windows over other operating systems despite its shortcomings: AutoHotKey makes customization trivially easy. He walks through progressively complex scripts he's written, from simple mouse button remapping to automated sci-hub paper retrieval. The key insight is that while similar functionality might be achievable on Mac or Linux, the friction of implementation matters enormously. When adding affordances is easy, you actually add them; when it requires diving into OS internals, you don't bother.
key_points: AutoHotKey hooks into the entire Windows system, making it easy to augment workflows without understanding OS internals
Small affordances compound: the difference between 'a single hotkey' and 'four steps' dramatically affects whether you'll actually use something
Muscle memory matters for productivity - alt+tab fails because the window stack is unpredictable, but fixed hotkeys work reliably
The tool's accessibility trumps its elegance - AHK's language is admittedly ugly but the low barrier to entry makes customization actually happen
Portability of scripts beats registry hacks or system-level configurations that don't transfer to new machines
money_quotes: There's something about the difference between 'a single hotkey' and 'four steps' that makes me more likely to bother.
When adding affordances to my computer is easy, I'm more likely to add them.
Most my AutoHotKey uses are like this: tacking on a crude API on to something that doesn't have one.
As a hard-core Vim user, anything that gets in the way of muscle memory is bad and should be avoided.
I wouldn't call it particularly elegant, and it's filled with tons of redundancy and quirks. Even its fans admit how nasty the language can be.
Using AutoHotKey makes me feel like I can customize my machine. My scripts bring the computer closer to what I need.
posts.key_insight: The friction of customization matters more than the possibility of customization - when adding small workflow improvements is easy, you actually do it; when it requires deep technical knowledge, you don't.

posts.summary: The post explores two approaches to data validation: predicative (using boolean functions to filter valid items from all representable states) and constructive (structuring data so only valid states can be created). While the constructive approach of 'making illegal states unrepresentable' is often seen as obviously superior, Wayne argues it has significant limitations including ergonomic costs, difficulty handling overlapping categories, and poor support for nonmonotonic properties. He concludes that most real-world data will be a mix of both approaches, and that finding good constructive solutions is a learnable skill with identifiable patterns.
key_points: Predicative approaches use boolean functions to validate a subset of representable data, while constructive approaches structure data so only valid states can exist
Constructive data is sound but not complete—it guarantees validity but may not represent all valid states or represent them evenly
Nonmonotonic properties (those that can be falsified by adding information) are particularly difficult to handle constructively
The distinction between 'invalid' and 'undesirable' data creates tension with purely constructive approaches, since programs often need to represent undesirable states to fix them
Finding constructive solutions is a skill that can be improved through recognizing patterns like finite enumerations, wrapper types, and dictionary inversions
money_quotes: We consciously make the choice to make illegal states unrepresentable and should study how we make that choice.
Finding a good constructive solution requires that you be 'clever' in a way finding a predicative solution does not.
Constructive systems are very bad at negating properties, in the same way that most sound static type systems cannot type 'everything that is not an integer.'
Data should never be invalid, but sometimes data is in a valid but undesirable state. Oftentimes the purpose of the program itself is to take undesirable data and move it out of the undesired state.
If finding constructive solutions is a skill, then it is a skill we can improve.
I'm also fascinated by how 'finding constructive solutions' can be seen as a skill with patterns and techniques, while this is less true with finding predicative solutions.
posts.key_insight: Making illegal states unrepresentable is not universally superior—it's a tradeoff that works well for monotonic properties but struggles with negation, overlapping categories, and the need to represent undesirable-but-valid states.

posts.summary: This tutorial addresses one of the two hard parts of property-based testing: generating complex, interdependent inputs. Using an exam/exam-instance example, it walks through Hypothesis strategies from simple (type inference, filter, assume) to sophisticated (builds, composite, data). The key challenge is generating data with preconditions and dependencies—like ensuring an ExamInstance's answers match its Exam's length. Composite strategies emerge as the most powerful tool, allowing multiple draws to be related to each other. The tutorial emphasizes that generating valid inputs by construction is better than filtering invalid ones.
key_points: Type inference with from_type works for simple cases but generates too many nonsensical inputs for real testing
filter and assume are easy but wasteful—you're still generating bad inputs and throwing them away
The builds strategy lets you construct objects with constrained field generators, ensuring valid inputs by construction
composite strategies are the key to generating interdependent data where one value constrains another
The data strategy allows interactive drawing within tests but sacrifices some Hypothesis features like better error reporting
money_quotes: Once you learn the basics, there are two hard parts to writing property-based tests: What are good properties to test? How do I generate complex inputs?
The more narrowly we can constrain the search space the more likely we are to get interesting edge cases.
We're better off writing a customized generator that always generates good inputs.
The inner function does not return a strategy. It returns regular Python values. The @composite decorator is what converts this function into a strategy.
Hypothesis provides a lot of mechanisms to create complex data. Learning how to use them well is a bit of an art, but well worth it.
posts.key_insight: Generating valid test inputs by construction through composite strategies is more effective than filtering invalid random inputs, and is essential for testing code with interdependent data structures.

posts.summary: This post explains the distinction between weak and strong fairness in TLA+ specifications, concepts that determine whether concurrent systems are guaranteed to make progress. Fairness addresses whether actions that can happen will eventually happen, preventing specifications from allowing infinite 'stuttering' where systems freeze. Weak fairness guarantees an action happens if it's permanently enabled, while strong fairness guarantees it happens if it's intermittently enabled. The post uses clock and worker retry examples to illustrate how unfair, weakly fair, and strongly fair systems behave differently. Wayne notes that even production systems like Microsoft's thread scheduler opt for weak rather than strong fairness for practical tradeoffs.
key_points: TLA+ specifications are stutter-invariant, meaning systems can infinitely pause without violating safety properties, but this blocks liveness properties
Weak fairness guarantees an action happens if it's always enabled; strong fairness guarantees it happens if it's repeatedly (but not continuously) enabled
Fairness applies to specific actions rather than entire specs, allowing fine-grained control over which parts of a system must make progress
Strong fairness is considered less important because weak fairness is safer to assume and more realistically achievable in practice
Real systems like Microsoft's thread scheduler deliberately choose weak over strong fairness to avoid problems like lock convoys
money_quotes: If I do not explicitly say that I cannot throw the clock in a lake, the spec permits me to throw the clock in a lake.
Stutter-invariance is what makes composing specs in TLA+ a miserable experience, as opposed to other specification languages, where composing specs is a punishment for sinners in hell.
A system is unfair if an infinite sequence of stutter steps is a valid behavior. This is equivalent to no agents in the system ever making progress.
An action is weakly fair if, if it always can happen, then it eventually will happen.
Strong fairness says that if an action isn't permanently disabled it will eventually happen.
They cannot guarantee that all threads make progress, but they make it probabilistically near-certain, and in return they avoid lock convoys.
posts.key_insight: The distinction between weak and strong fairness determines whether a concurrent system must guarantee progress for actions that are permanently enabled versus those that are only intermittently enabled, with real-world systems often choosing weaker guarantees for practical reasons.

posts.summary: The post introduces Frink, a programming language designed specifically for handling units of measurement with dimensional analysis. Wayne explains why units are surprisingly complex—different measurement systems, historical variations, ambiguous dimensions, and uncertainties all create challenges that most programming languages handle poorly. Frink strikes a balance between the strict compile-time checking of F# and the simple conversion tool GNU units, offering runtime flexibility with built-in dimensional analysis. The language includes over 5,000 predefined units, natural mathematical syntax, and affordances like interval arithmetic and timestamp handling. Wayne demonstrates its practical value by calculating the calories in homemade chocolates, showing how Frink handles mixed units and uncertainty naturally.
key_points: Units are far more complex than they appear: historical definitions change, dimensions can be ambiguous (energy vs torque), and different systems (SI, American, Gaussian) create conversion nightmares
Most programming languages punt on units, relegating them to libraries; F# has built-in support but requires explicit conversions that add boilerplate
Frink uses natural math syntax where spaces are multiplication, making equation translation easy while preventing incompatible dimension additions
The language includes practical affordances like interval arithmetic for uncertainty, timestamp handling with timezone conversion, and 5,000+ predefined units
Frink fills a niche between strict industrial software (F#) and simple conversion tools (GNU units), optimized for interactive exploratory calculation
money_quotes: One day Alan Eliasen read a fart joke and got so mad he invented a programming language.
This is the bug that destroyed the Mars Climate Orbiter.
If I'm trying to find the Titanic's weight in cups of rice I don't want to spend ten minutes writing unit conversions.
The syntax is designed to look like 'natural' math, so a space counts as a multiplication.

I think the candela is a scam, and I am completely opposed to it. Some good-for-nothing lighting 'engineers' or psychologists probably got this perceptually-rigged abomination into the whole otherwise scientific endeavor.
Different dimensions doesn't always mean the units are incompatible.
Frink treats intervals as a single unknown value inside the interval, not the range itself. This means that for any interval x, x - x = 0.
posts.key_insight: Unit handling in software is a deceptively complex problem that most languages ignore, but Frink demonstrates that a purpose-built language can make dimensional analysis both rigorous and pleasant to use interactively.

posts.summary: Hillel Wayne presents a practical improvement to traditional cheatsheets for teaching formal methods workshops. He identifies that standard dense cheatsheets overwhelm students who don't yet have mental models to navigate them, leading to two failure modes: using wrong concepts or being unable to locate relevant information. His solution is 'progressive cheatsheets' where students receive increasingly complete versions as they progress through the material. A key refinement ensures layout consistency across all versions by rendering hidden content as white text rather than removing it, preventing students from having to relearn content locations.
key_points: Dense cheatsheets fail learners who don't know what they're looking for - they either misapply advanced concepts or can't find relevant material amid the noise
Progressive cheatsheets reveal content incrementally, with each version containing only material covered so far
Layout consistency matters: content should appear in the same position across all versions to avoid accidental cognitive overhead
The clever implementation trick of rendering future content as white text (rather than removing it) maintains consistent layout while hiding irrelevant material
Practical testing at workshops showed fewer false negatives where students tried to use wrong concepts
money_quotes: The more information you pack into a single page, the more likely someone's going to find what they need… if they already know what they need.
They don't yet have a mental model, so they don't know what material is relevant and what isn't.
It's extra mental overhead for the students that has nothing to do with the actual material. Accidental difficulty, not essential difficulty. I want it out.

LaTeX, a typesetting DSL that grew wildly beyond its original niche. It's the best tool for fine control over layouts. This is less because of its own intrinsic quality and more because everything else is far, far worse.
Cheatsheets should be at most one page. Past two and nobody looks at them.
So I cheated.
posts.key_insight: Cheatsheets optimized for experts fail learners because density helps only those who already know what they're looking for - progressive revelation matches the reference material to the student's evolving mental model.

posts.summary: This post explores how J (a successor to APL) embodies Kenneth Iverson's idea that better notation leads to deeper mathematical insights. Rather than focusing on APL's infamous terse syntax, the author demonstrates how treating arrays as primitives—rather than collections of scalars—fundamentally changes how we think about programming. Through examples of rank manipulation, sieves for filtering, and grades for sorting, the post shows that APL's essence lies in its conceptual model of array transformations, not its cryptic symbols. The author argues that learning J made them a better programmer by teaching them to think about operations on entire arrays at once.
key_points: In J/APL, arrays are primitives rather than collections of scalars—operations naturally work on entire arrays without explicit loops or maps
Rank allows precise control over which dimension of a multidimensional array an operation acts on, enabling flexible data reorganization
Sieves (boolean arrays from comparisons) are first-class values that can be manipulated, inverted, or applied to different arrays independently of filtering
Grades (permutation vectors for sorting) capture the mathematical essence of sorting and can be stored, inverted, or analyzed as data
The essence of APL isn't its terse syntax but its conceptual model of treating array transformations as fundamental primitives
money_quotes: Changing how we describe programs changes how we think about them.
Instead of having arrays as 'collections of scalars', the array is the primitive.
J arrays are purely structural collections of data, not conceptual ones.
Rank helps us think about our script as a collection of executions, not just individual ones.
The sieve is a distinct value.
Our means of filtering becomes itself data.
'Return the permutation that is sorted' is the simplest mathematical definition of 'sorting'.
The essence of APL is in how it thinks about arrays as primitives and how it represents array transformations.
posts.key_insight: APL's power comes not from its notorious syntax but from treating arrays as primitives and making transformations like filtering and sorting into first-class, manipulable data structures.

posts.summary: This post provides a comprehensive guide to decision tables, a specification tool that maps finite enumerated inputs to outputs. Wayne explains that decision tables must be complete (covering all input combinations) and sound (no conflicting outputs) to be valid, making them a form of formal specification. He distinguishes decision tables from pattern matching by emphasizing they work at any abstraction level for design, not just code. The bulk of the post covers techniques to manage table bloat, including wildcards, decomposition, and handling dependent inputs and intervals. Wayne concludes by identifying when decision tables become inappropriate—complex data structures, time-based decisions, or when formalism doesn't add clarity.
key_points: Decision tables are formal specifications that map all possible input combinations to exactly one output, enabling automated correctness analysis
The 'any' wildcard (-) compresses tables by representing multiple rows but can introduce soundness errors or implicit ordering
Infinite states can be represented through equivalence classes or intervals, converting unbounded inputs into finite enumerable values
Dependent inputs (where one restricts another's values) require careful handling—distinguish between facts that can't be false and assertions that shouldn't be false
Decision tables hit their limits with complex data structures, time-based decisions, or when completeness isn't visually obvious
money_quotes: Decision tables are easy, simple, and powerful. You can teach them in five minutes and write one in half that time.
Pattern matching is part of the language implementation. Decision tables are more to help you with the design.
By being formalized, errors in the construction of the table often map to errors in our design itself.
Ideally is a dangerous word.
A fact is something that cannot possibly be false. If we run into a case where it is false, then a software bug is the least of our worries.
If you're using the decision table as a mental augmentation tool, then taking shortcuts is fine. The goal is to help you think better and excessive formalism doesn't always lead to insight.
While they have a low expressiveness ceiling, there's no opportunity cost to trying them first.
posts.key_insight: Decision tables are low-cost formal specifications that help catch design errors by forcing explicit enumeration of all input-output mappings, but their simplicity means they're best suited for discrete, independent inputs rather than complex state or temporal logic.

posts.summary: This post demonstrates how to use TLA+ to model and verify a Go concurrency bug originally described by Chris Siebenmann. The author walks through translating Go code with goroutines, channels, and defer statements into a PlusCal specification, showing how to model both buffered and unbuffered channels. The TLA+ model checker successfully reproduces the deadlock and verifies that three proposed fixes all work correctly. The post concludes by noting that while TLA+ works for this purpose, Spin might be better suited for Go channel semantics since it has channels as a native datatype.
key_points: Go's unbuffered channels require special handling in TLA+ because they need both sender and receiver to be ready simultaneously, which doesn't map cleanly to PlusCal's single-process-per-step model
Goroutine spawning can be emulated in TLA+ by pre-defining all processes with an 'initialized' flag that they await before executing
The deadlock occurs because goroutines can only return tokens after writing to the unbuffered 'found' channel, but main only reads from 'found' after initializing all goroutines
About 75 lines of TLA+ specification verified 20 lines of Go code, with over half being reusable channel logic
Spin may be more appropriate than TLA+ for modeling Go channels since it has channels as a native datatype and was previously used to model Go's runtime scheduler
money_quotes: But just knowing the syntax isn't enough to write specs, and it helps to have examples to draw from.

For the purposes of pedagogy I'm not modeling what we actually read or write. This is good practice when writing real-world specs too: write the simplest specification that usefully captures behavior and iteratively add detail to that.
The goroutines can't return tokens until all goroutines are initialized, and main can't initialize all goroutines until some goroutines have returned their tokens.
Noneless, writing the TLA+ version wouldn't be significantly more effort than writing the original version and could save you net time if it caught the deadlock before production.
Obviously I'm inclined towards using TLA+, given it's my professional language of choice. However, I suspect that TLA+ isn't exactly the right tool for modeling Go channels.
posts.key_insight: Formal specification with TLA+ can catch subtle concurrency bugs like deadlocks before they reach production, and the effort to write specs is comparable to writing the original code while being reusable across similar problems.

posts.summary: Wayne argues that the reason programmers don't adopt software correctness techniques isn't laziness or apathy, but inconvenience. He draws an analogy to flossing: everyone knows root canals are worse than buggy software, yet most people don't floss daily because it's fiddly and annoying. The same dynamic applies to TDD, type systems, and formal methods. He points to Ruby's rspec ecosystem and Elm/TypeScript's user-friendly tooling as evidence that convenience drives adoption. Wayne concludes that communities wanting mainstream adoption should focus on improving UI/UX, documentation, and tooling rather than blaming outsiders for not caring about correctness.
key_points: Every correctness community (TDD, types, formal methods) accuses others of not caring about correctness, revealing a symmetrical blind spot
People don't avoid correctness tools because they don't care, but because the tools are inconvenient—just like people skip flossing despite knowing root canals are worse
Languages like Elm and TypeScript succeed partly through better error messages and IDE integration, serving as gateways to more rigorous systems
Expert blindness, academic incentive structures, and the distinct skill set required for UI/UX work all contribute to tools remaining inconvenient
Low-hanging fruit like documentation, cookbooks, and higher-level abstractions can shift adoption without fundamental tool changes
money_quotes: If people are unwilling to do something simple to keep their teeth from rotting, why should we expect people to use annoying inconvenient tools to improve software?
Experienced people are willing to tolerate more inconvenience for more power.
When inconvenience isn't addressed—or even acknowledged—in the community then it's a signal that the community doesn't care about growth.
One reason I don't like the 'developers don't care' excuse is that it's too nihilistic.
A lot of those 'apathetic' developers started using formal methods once learning it was easier.
Academics can't publish papers about how they made a tool easier to use, even if improving usability would dramatically improve industry adoption.
For communities who want to go mainstream, it's a mistake to blame outsiders for not seeing the light. Maybe you just have a grimy window.
posts.key_insight: The adoption gap for software correctness tools is primarily a usability problem, not a developer apathy problem, and it's within our power to fix.

posts.summary: This post demonstrates how to use PRISM, a probabilistic model checker, to analyze task queue performance. The author builds progressively complex models showing that while throughput scales linearly with workers (2x workers ≈ 2x throughput), latency behaves counterintuitively—growing quadratically with task volume for a single worker. Adding a second worker doesn't just halve latency; it fundamentally changes the growth pattern from quadratic to sublinear because queue jams require both workers to stall simultaneously. The post walks through the PRISM specification language, explains how to model both throughput and latency using reward functions, and concludes by generalizing the model using binomial expansion to handle arbitrary numbers of workers.
key_points: Latency in task queues grows quadratically with volume for a single worker—10x more tasks means 40x more total wait time
Adding a second worker changes latency growth from quadratic to sublinear, not merely halving it
Queue jams occur when slow tasks block faster ones from processing, and more tasks means both higher jam probability and more tasks piling up behind jams
PRISM's probabilistic model checking can reveal counterintuitive system behaviors through formal specification rather than just tracing/metrics
The binomial expansion provides an elegant way to generalize worker processing probabilities to N workers
money_quotes: A 10x increase in volume gives us a 40x increase in total latency!
