The latency spike then comes from two places: first, more tasks means a higher chance of a long task that jams the queue. Second, if the queue gets jammed there are more tasks that can pile up behind it, adding latency.
For one worker the wait time was just about quadratic, while this is sublinear. The queue only jams if *both* workers stall out, which is a lot less likely.

For all their popularity, we don't abstractly model the properties of task queues. We have tools to implement them and tools to trace/metric them, but we don't use theoretical models to understand our systems in abstract.
With specifications, we have two levels. We write the specs and then we write the properties about the specs.
This was a simple model of task queues. We didn't cover priority, error handling, multiple queues, etc. Nonetheless, the spec was good enough to show how nonlinear latency is.
posts.key_insight: Adding workers to a task queue doesn't just improve throughput linearly—it fundamentally changes the mathematical behavior of latency from quadratic to sublinear growth.

posts.summary: Hillel Wayne explores two computing history puzzles—why Vim uses hjkl for navigation and why JavaScript months are 0-indexed—to illustrate how historical explanations have layers. The surface answers (home row efficiency, array indexing convenience) are post-hoc rationalizations. Digging deeper reveals that hjkl was inherited from the ADM-3A terminal's control key conventions rooted in ASCII bit manipulation, while 0-indexed months came from early Unix memory optimization on the PDP-7. Wayne argues that understanding these deeper layers reveals the actual forces that shaped our tools, though each layer requires exponentially more research effort to uncover.
key_points: Post-hoc rationalizations for design choices often sound reasonable but miss the actual historical causes
The ADM-3A used hjkl for arrows because ^H and ^J were already ASCII control characters for cursor movement, making ^K and ^L natural choices for up and right
JavaScript's 0-indexed months trace back to early Unix developers doing pointer arithmetic to save memory on systems with only kilobytes of RAM
The day-of-month vs day-of-year indexing inconsistency makes sense when you realize one was for display and one for computation
Each layer of historical explanation takes considerably more effort to excavate than the layer before
money_quotes: When asked why something is the way it is, most people will give a post-hoc rationalization.
If you look a little into the past, you often see that 'things are this way because they were this way'.
And if you look deeper, you see the forces that lead to things becoming that way.
People see the first layer and assume that's all there is. This makes history seem irrelevant.
A decent computer in the 1970s might have a few kilobytes of memory. If you tried to store all of the month names in memory, that could be almost 10% of your total RAM!
Lost knowledge found again.
posts.key_insight: What appears to be deliberate design is often inherited convention, and understanding the deeper historical layers reveals the actual constraints and forces that shaped our tools.

posts.summary: Hillel Wayne investigates whether software engineering deserves to be called 'engineering' by interviewing 17 professionals who worked as traditional engineers before becoming software developers. He systematically dismantles common arguments against the title—that software lacks math, high consequences, or licensing—showing that these don't define engineering in other fields either. Of the crossovers interviewed, 15 considered software engineering to be real engineering. Wayne concludes that while not all software developers are engineers, the gap between software development and software engineering is much smaller than in traditional fields, separated by circumstance rather than essence.
key_points: Nobody in the 'is software engineering real engineering' debate has actually worked as a traditional engineer, making their arguments based on stereotypes rather than experience
Software uses discrete math (graph theory, logic, combinatorics) rather than continuous math—different branches, not absence of math
Engineering licensure is a political/social construct that varies by state and country, not an essential quality of engineering itself
15 of 17 crossover engineers interviewed considered software engineering to be real engineering
The gap between 'software craft' and 'software engineering' is much smaller than between electrician and electrical engineer—we're separated by circumstance, not essence
money_quotes: My personal blog has better security than some $100 million mining projects.
They've never built a bridge.
Just because there are no integrals doesn't mean we are mathless.
Regulations are written in blood.
Whether or not we are engineers is irrelevant to whether or not we are good engineers.
Something becomes engineering if enough engineers say it's engineering.
We are separated from engineering by circumstance, not by essence, and we can choose to bridge that gap at will.
If chemical engineering started now, people would refuse to call it engineering. And they'd be wrong to refuse.
posts.key_insight: The debate over whether software engineering is 'real' engineering has been conducted entirely by people who've never worked as traditional engineers; those who have crossed over from traditional engineering mostly say yes, it counts.

posts.summary: Hillel Wayne argues that software engineering is not fundamentally different from traditional engineering disciplines, despite common beliefs. Through interviews with crossover engineers (people who worked in both software and traditional engineering), he systematically dismantles claims that software uniquely requires Agile, is more unpredictable, or is less rigorous. While acknowledging real differences—software's consistency, faster iteration velocity, and softer constraints—he concludes these don't make software 'special' but rather just another engineering discipline with its own challenges, similar to how chemical and civil engineering each have unique concerns.
key_points: Traditional engineering also uses iterative, Agile-like approaches; Waterfall was never as dominant or rigid as software engineers believe
Software engineers vastly overestimate how predictable traditional engineering is—every field faces shifting requirements, unknown unknowns, and on-the-ground improvisation
The 'code is design' distinction breaks down because design and construction aren't disjoint in any engineering field; construction reveals design issues and changes the design
Software's real differences are consistency (code doesn't wear out or vary like physical materials), velocity (changes can happen in seconds vs. weeks), and softer constraints
Every engineering field is siloed and ignorant of others; software's belief in its uniqueness is partly a defense mechanism to justify not following engineering practices
money_quotes: To assume that software is uniquely unpredictable is a special kind of arrogance.
We are not special. Almost everything we think is unique about software appears in every other field of engineering.
If I give you a sorting function, you can expect it to sort. You do not expect it to sort a given list of numbers only 95% of the time. That would be ridiculous.

There's like a 1,000,000 million times more checks and balances in software than in traditional engineering. [...] there's days that I am shocked skyscrapers don't fall over daily and planes don't crash.
The software engineer is being squeezed by the rest of the team. They're usually asked to save everyone's bacon.
Software engineers can undo their kludges. Trad engineers cannot.
Nobody ever gets a chance to leave their bubble.
posts.key_insight: Software engineering's supposed uniqueness is largely a self-serving myth; we share the same fundamental challenges as all engineering disciplines, and recognizing this opens opportunities for cross-disciplinary learning.

posts.summary: This essay concludes Wayne's 'Crossover Project' exploring what software engineers can learn from traditional engineers and vice versa. Through interviews with engineers who crossed between fields, Wayne finds that traditional engineers excel at planning, requirements gathering, and professional responsibility, while software engineers have developed superior open communities and revolutionary version control systems. The key insight is that these differences aren't essential tradeoffs—both disciplines could adopt each other's strengths. Wayne argues software engineers should spend more time thinking before coding, while traditional engineering could benefit enormously from our collaborative culture and tools like git.
key_points: Traditional engineers are better at strategic planning and requirements gathering, while software engineers excel at day-to-day process and tooling
Software's lack of physicality makes it harder to feel responsible for its impact, contributing to less professional rigor
Version control is described as the single most revolutionary tool unique to software that could transform traditional engineering
Software's open culture (practitioner conferences, free learning materials, open source tools) enables career mobility and cross-pollination that traditional engineering lacks
The solution to imperfect plans is flexible dynamic plans, not abandoning planning entirely
money_quotes: The response to plans being imperfect is to make flexible dynamic plans, not to throw away planning entirely.
When even simple statecharts are decried as Big Design Up Front, are we really where we should be?
Version control is the single most innovative, most revolutionary, most paradigm-shifting tool that is uniquely ours.
It's easier to wave off a frustrating bug with 'oh, that's just computers being bad' as opposed to 'we did something wrong'.
I learned more in the first two hours of interviewing than I had than from all the thought leader essays on the subject.
There's nothing like Deconstruct or Pycon or !!con, a practitioner-oriented conference run solely for the joy of the craft.
The rest are still saving files as form-draft-3.docx.
posts.key_insight: Software and traditional engineering have each optimized for different aspects of the craft—we should adopt each other's strengths rather than assuming inherent tradeoffs prevent it.

posts.summary: This post explains TLA+ action properties, which are properties that apply to state transitions rather than individual states. Unlike invariants that must hold for every state, action properties validate pairs of states—ensuring transitions are valid even when both states are individually acceptable. The post provides the syntax `[][A]_x` for expressing these properties while respecting TLA+'s stutter invariance. Wayne positions this as 'intermediate-level' content filling a gap between introductions and case studies, offering practical patterns for conditional properties, state machines, and various real-world constraints.
key_points: Action properties validate transitions between states, not individual states—useful when both states are valid but the transition is not
The `[][A]_x` syntax handles stutter invariance by meaning 'A holds OR x is unchanged'
Conditional action properties using `=>` enable powerful patterns like 'if X changes, it must be because of action Y'
State transition tables can be encoded as action properties to constrain valid state machine behavior
Action properties are more common than liveness properties but less critical to spec validity
money_quotes: There's not a whole lot on TLA+ technique out there: all the resources are either introductions or case studies. Good for people starting out, bad for people past that.
Going from `x = 2` to `x = 1` would violate this, but those are both individually valid states. It's only the transition that is invalid.
Invariants and action properties only cover bad states and transitions. They don't cover 'the system actually does what you need it to do.' That's liveness.
Action properties are very useful, but not critical in the same way.
Action properties can only describe pairs of states. You can't natively write a property that spans three states without adding an auxiliary variable.
TLC can check `[][Next]_vars` just like any other action property! This is the launching point into refinement, or using an entire specification as the checked property of another spec.
posts.key_insight: Action properties fill the gap between state invariants and liveness properties by validating transitions—catching invalid changes between states that are individually acceptable.

posts.summary: Hillel Wayne investigates why tech companies ask linked list questions in interviews, using historical research methods to trace their origins. Through interviewing older programmers, analyzing 1980s Usenet archives, and examining job postings, he builds evidence that these questions originated as practical tests for C and Pascal experience—languages that required manual pointer manipulation. The practice persisted and spread through influential figures like Joel Spolsky and Gayle Laakmann McDowell, who exported questions from low-level companies like Microsoft and Google. Ironically, the original context was lost, and people now justify these questions with post-hoc rationalizations about 'CS fundamentals' or 'quick thinking.'
key_points: The two common justifications for linked list questions—testing CS fundamentals vs. testing reasoning ability—are contradictory, suggesting the real reason has been forgotten
In the 1980s, C and Pascal were dominant languages that uniquely required manual linked list implementation, making these questions practical experience tests rather than theoretical ones
Analysis of 1980s Usenet shows C programmers discussed pointers in 16.5% of posts vs only 3% for Lisp, Smalltalk, and Prolog programmers
Joel Spolsky's 'Guerilla Guide to Interviewing' and 'Cracking the Coding Interview' spread these questions to companies where they weren't relevant
The questions have become self-defeating: you can no longer test C experience because people learn linked lists specifically to pass interviews
money_quotes: Two contradictory answers tells me there's some history involved.
People record only a fraction in writing of what they care about, so memories are the richest source of cultural context.
Secondary sources are great for learning history but bad for doing history.
'Reverse this linked list' isn't a test of algorithm knowledge or 'thinking on your feet'. It's a test of if you've programmed C.
Companies weren't testing for theoretical CS knowledge, they were testing for practical experience with pointers.

Ironically, this also made LL questions useless for their original purpose. You can't test if someone is good at C by asking them to reverse a linked list. They might know how to reverse linked lists because they needed to learn to pass interviews!
Even things as mundane as the interview questions we ask can have rich stories underneath.
posts.key_insight: Linked list interview questions began as practical tests for C programming experience in the 1980s, but lost their original context and became cargo-culted as 'CS fundamentals' tests despite being irrelevant to most modern programming work.

posts.summary: The post explores why formal specifications don't compose well, walking through increasingly complex attempts to combine two simple 'blinker' specs in Linear Temporal Logic. Starting with naive conjunction, each attempt reveals a new obstacle: synchronization constraints, the frame problem, fairness violations, and stuttering requirements. Even after making specs stutter-invariant to recover some composability, introducing shared state between specs causes deadlock-like failures. The conclusion is that specs must totally describe how their variables change, and component specs don't know about each other's variables, forcing practitioners to manually stitch specs together rather than compose them modularly.
key_points: Naive conjunction of specs forces synchronization, while disjunction loses initialization guarantees — neither preserves the properties we want
The frame problem means that when one spec's action is skipped, unmentioned variables can take arbitrary values unless explicitly held constant
Stutter-invariance (allowing no-op steps) recovers composability for independent specs but sacrifices boundedness properties and expressiveness
Dependent specs sharing variables fail because each spec must totally describe its variables' behavior, leading to contradictory requirements that permanently disable one spec
The practical result is that formal methods communities tend to write large global specifications rather than composing smaller modular ones
money_quotes: Beginners to formal specification expect specifications should be organized like programs: multiple independent modules that interact through public interfaces, where the modules don't know about each others' private implementations.
The two blinkers are always synchronous. One can't run faster than the other, or consistently blink slightly earlier than the other, etc. This is rarely what we want when we compose specs.
Since BlinkY doesn't have x in its 'frame', it doesn't restrict x to sensible values.
We've gotten composability back! This even allows both sync and async blinkers. We just had to lose boundedness properties and significantly restrict our expressiveness. Hooray!
By sharing a flag between the two specs, we have permanently disabled one of them.
Composing specs is hard because specifications need to totally describe how their variables change, and component specs don't know about each other's variables.

This doesn't mean combining specs needs to be intrinsically hard: we can come up with new techniques to help stitch them together. But it means that there's a predilection in the communities to write large global specs, not combine smaller ones.
posts.key_insight: Specifications resist modular composition because each spec must totally describe the behavior of all its variables, and combining specs that share state inevitably produces contradictory requirements that can only be resolved by manually examining and integrating their internals.

posts.summary: Hillel Wayne presents a more intuitive way to understand the Liskov Substitution Principle: if a class inherits from another, it should pass all of the parent's black-box tests. The traditional approach uses three rules (method contracts, class invariants, and the history rule), but these feel arbitrary and disconnected. The testing approach unifies all three rules under one simple concept. Research by Baniassad and Summers found this pedagogical reframing significantly improved student understanding of LSP, taking them from failing to passing grades on related questions.
key_points: The traditional LSP rules (don't strengthen preconditions, don't weaken postconditions, preserve invariants, follow history rule) are hard to remember and feel like ad-hoc patches
The history rule seems like a crude hack because contracts and invariants only span one method's lifetime, but tests can call sequences of methods
Reframing LSP as 'must pass all supertype black-box tests' provides a unified explanation for all the traditional rules
This testing approach also helps students understand the distinction between black-box and white-box testing
The research showed dramatic improvement in student comprehension, from failing to passing grades on LSP questions
money_quotes: If X inherits from Y, then X should pass all of Y's black box tests.
The history rule always felt like a tack-on. It makes me worry that we're just patching up holes as we find them.
We originally needed the history rule because contracts and invariants only span the lifetime of one method. But now we can just call a sequence of methods in one test!
For a class to be substitutable for its supertype, it must pass all the supertype's black box tests.
Students went from not demonstrating any intuition around the rule, to 'getting it'.
It all just makes me lose trust in the applicability of LSP.
posts.key_insight: The Liskov Substitution Principle becomes intuitive when reframed as a testing concept: a subtype must pass all black-box tests written for its parent type.

posts.summary: This post collects Hillel Wayne's impressions of talks he attended at the final Strangeloop 2023 conference. He covers topics ranging from career advice and play in engineering to LLMs, stack-based languages, comedy with generative models, permacomputing sustainability, formal methods accessibility, and teaching difficult concepts. The post concludes with a personal reflection on how Strangeloop shaped his career and the unique community it fostered. Wayne notes that while a successor conference might emerge, it cannot replicate what made Strangeloop special—it was fundamentally Alex Miller's vision.
key_points: Stack-based languages have elegant properties: programs naturally compose since they're functions of type Stack -> Stack, and stack operations are associative regardless of how you partition the program
The tension in permacomputing lies between sustainability ideals and practical achievability—there's a spectrum between extreme minimalism and wasteful consumption that remains undefined
Making formal methods accessible is crucial; new languages like Fault aim to lower the barrier to entry for reasoning about systems
The best teaching insight from the conference: 'behind every best practice is a gruesome story'
Strangeloop's unique value was its eclectic mix of academia, industry, art, and activism—something difficult to replicate because it was one person's specific vision
money_quotes: Between the 'hurr dur you must be in it for the passion' people and the 'hurr durr smash capitalism' people, it's nice to hear some acknowledgement that money makes your life nice.

Stack ops are associative: if you chop a stack program into subprograms and pipe them into each other, it doesn't matter where you make the cuts, you still get the same final stack. That's really, really cool.
I'm painfully aware of just how hard grokking FM can be, and anything that makes it more accessible is good.
When she said that the hardest part is output formatting I felt it in my soul.
Behind every best practice is a gruesome story.
I consider myself a very good teacher. This talk made me a better one.
There are lots of people can run a good conference, but there's only one person who can run Alex's conference.
Whatever comes next will be fundamentally different. Still good, I'm sure, but different.
posts.key_insight: Great conferences, like great software, reflect a singular vision that cannot be replicated—only honored through something fundamentally different.

posts.summary: This post introduces a conceptual framework from Michael Jackson for thinking about formal specifications: the distinction between 'machine' (the part of the system you control) and 'world' (everything you don't). Using a TLA+ bank transfer example with a race condition bug, Hillel demonstrates that solutions must work within the machine's boundaries—you can add a lock to serialize transactions, but you can't control how people use their checkbooks. The post elaborates on observability (which states each domain can read/write) and distinguishes between internal properties (implementation details) and observable properties (what users actually experience). The key insight is that observable properties matter more because they're what actually affects people.
key_points: The machine is what you control; the world is everything else—and this boundary shifts depending on your scope in the system
You need to model the whole system but you're only designing the machine, so world behavior can be abstracted to just how it affects your component
Observable properties (like NoOverdrafts) matter more than internal properties (like OnlyOneWithdraw) because they're what actually affects users
If the world can break an invariant, it's not really an invariant—you need resilience instead
Specs often break not because the machine has a bug, but because the surrounding world has changed
money_quotes: The machine is the part of the system you have direct control over and the world is all the parts that you don't.
One way you can't solve it is by forcing people to deposit one cheque at a time. You don't have any control over what people do with their chequebook!
If they're sending you bad data, you need to faithfully model receiving that bad data in your spec as part of designing your machine.
While you need to model the whole system, you're only designing the machine.
If a property isn't observable, it doesn't have any connection to the broader world, so nobody is actually affected by it breaking.
If the world can break an invariant, it's not an invariant.
It's not uncommon for a spec to break not because the machine has a bug, but because the surrounding world has changed.
posts.key_insight: When designing systems, you must faithfully model the world you can't control while only implementing the machine you can—and observable properties that affect users matter far more than internal implementation details.

posts.summary: Hillel Wayne explores a mental random number generator algorithm originally posted by George Marsaglia on Usenet. The technique involves starting with a 2-digit seed, then repeatedly computing a new number by adding the tens digit to 6 times the units digit. Wayne investigates why this works using Raku, discovering that only certain multipliers (like 6, 11, 18) produce a single orbit covering all values. He explains the connection to multiply-with-carry and Lehmer RNGs, ultimately recommending 18 as a multiplier for better randomness despite requiring memorization of the 18 times-table.
key_points: The algorithm works by treating the mental calculation as a multiply-with-carry RNG, which is equivalent to a Lehmer RNG running in reverse
Only certain multipliers produce a single orbit - good choices include 6, 11, 18, and 50
Marsaglia chose 6 because it's easy to compute mentally and never produces 3-digit numbers
The multiplier 18 has every possible digit transition and a period of 178, making it more random but requiring learning the 18 times-table
The sequence will always slightly underrepresent 0 and 9 because the orbit excludes 10n-1 and 10n
money_quotes: Humans are notoriously bad at coming up with random numbers.
Raku, the language for gremlins.
If you picked 13 as your starting value, your random digits would be 3, 3, 3, 3, 3, 3.
I dunno, I kinda like an RNG that never gives me 3.
It's easier for me to mathematically reason about '10y mod 59' than 'multiply the last digit by six and add the first digit'.
I internalized it with maybe 10 minutes of practice.
The code I'm showing is less gremliny than the code I originally wrote. So just know it can be more gremlins than this.
posts.key_insight: A simple mental arithmetic trick for generating random digits is actually a multiply-with-carry RNG in disguise, and understanding the underlying math reveals which multipliers produce the best randomness.

posts.summary: Hillel Wayne introduces Picat's planner module, which extends logic programming by finding variable mutations that reach desired end states rather than just finding satisfying assignments. He demonstrates this through a grid pathfinding problem, progressively adding complexity: first basic movement, then multiple ordered goals, then optimal path finding across unordered goals. The planner elegantly handles each extension with minimal code changes, and can even combine with constraint solving for problems like finding the largest partitionable subset of numbers. Wayne concludes that while Picat isn't production-ready due to poor documentation and error messages, it excels as a toolkit language for specific computational problems.
key_points: Planning differs from standard logic programming by finding state mutations to reach goals, not just variable assignments that satisfy equations
The planner requires only three things: a starting state, action functions defining transitions, and a final state predicate
Adding features like multiple goals or optimal ordering requires surprisingly minimal code changes—often just one or two lines
Picat's planning integrates with its other features like constraint solving, enabling 'metaconstraint' problems
The language is best suited as a toolkit for specific problem classes rather than production use
money_quotes: Planning pushes this all one step further: instead of finding variable assignments that satisfy equations, we find variable *mutations* that reach a certain end state.
So Picat can 'choose' which goal it moves to next so as to minimize the overall path length.
This is all so mindblowing to me. It's almost like a metaconstraint solver, allowing me to express constraints *on the valid constraints*.
Picat seems more useful as a 'toolkit' language, one you learn to solve a specific class of computational problems, and where you're not expecting to maintain or share the code afterwards.
But hey it runs on Windows, which is better than 99% of research languages.
There's a handful of problems I struggled to do with regular programming languages and constraint solvers. Picat solves a lot of them quite elegantly.
posts.key_insight: Planning as a programming paradigm—finding sequences of state mutations to reach goals—offers an elegant middle ground between imperative programming and constraint solving that makes certain hard problems surprisingly tractable.

posts.summary: The post investigates why graphs, despite being ubiquitous in software (package dependencies, databases, state spaces, social networks), lack native support in mainstream programming languages. Through interviews with graph algorithm experts, library maintainers, and database engineers, Wayne identifies three interconnected reasons: the overwhelming number of graph type variations (directed, undirected, multigraphs, hypergraphs), the many possible internal representations (edge lists, adjacency matrices, etc.) each with different performance tradeoffs, and the critical importance of performance optimization for graph algorithms that are often NP-complete and run on enormous datasets. The conclusion is that the design space is simply too large and performance-sensitive for standard libraries to provide useful abstractions.
key_points: Graphs appear everywhere in software (dependencies, databases, state spaces, networks) yet almost no mainstream language provides built-in graph types
The design space is enormous: directed vs undirected, simple vs multi, hypergraphs, plus choices about node/edge data and identities
Different internal representations (edge list, adjacency list, adjacency matrix) have dramatically different performance characteristics for different operations and graph densities
Graph algorithm performance is so critical that generic implementations often can't compete with hand-rolled solutions optimized for specific use cases
NetworkX alone contains ~500 distinct graph algorithms in 60,000 lines of code, illustrating the maintenance burden a standard library would face
money_quotes: There is almost no graph support in any mainstream language. None have it as a built-in type, very few have them in the standard library, and many don't have a robust third-party library in the ecosystem.
The amount of things people want to do with graphs is absurd.
Every single implementation of pagerank that I compared to was wrong.
If you generate all the nodes, you've lost already.
A 'generic' graph implementation often doesn't cut it.
I was able to get working a proof of concept pretty quickly with [petgraph], but then… this is one of those cases where the performance constraints end up meeting reality.
It sometimes seems like graphs are too powerful, that all their possibilities are beyond my understanding.
posts.key_insight: The absence of graph types in programming languages isn't an oversight but a consequence of the impossibly large design space combined with performance requirements so stringent that generic abstractions consistently fail real-world use cases.

posts.summary: Hillel Wayne traces the etymology of the name 'Daniel' from its earliest known written appearance in Ugaritic texts from approximately 1350 BCE to its modern ubiquity. Starting from clay tablets discovered in the ruins of Ugarit, he follows the name through the Bronze Age Collapse, the Phoenician era, and into the Hebrew Bible. The investigation reveals that while the exact pronunciation may have shifted (from 'Danel' to 'Daniel'), the meaning 'judged by El/God' has remained consistent across 3,400 years. The name's survival into modernity is largely attributed to the Book of Daniel's importance in Christian theology, which kept it in mainstream use across cultures worldwide.
key_points: The name Daniel first appears in the Tale of Aqhat from Ugarit (~1350 BCE), meaning 'judged by El' - the same meaning it retains today
The Bronze Age Collapse destroyed Ugarit but shared Canaanite religious culture (including gods El and Baal Hadad) preserved the name through oral tradition
Ezekiel's reference to Daniel alongside Noah and Job as righteous non-Israelites suggests he was drawing on broader Canaanite folklore, not inventing a new figure
The spelling shifted from four letters (DN'L) to five letters (DNYL) in later Hebrew texts, raising questions about whether 'Danel' became 'Daniel'
The Book of Daniel's perceived prophecies about Jesus made the name important to Christians, ensuring its global spread over 2,000 years
money_quotes: A historian of the modern world has to learn how to sip from a firehose of evidence, while the historian of the ancient world must learn how to find water in the desert.
I grew up an Orthodox Jew, and it's extremely weird to study the actual history of your religion. Like vivisecting holy books.

I asked if any understood this text. I found out 1) that's like asking a person who 'can read ASCII' if they can understand Spanish, and 2) this is not actually cuneiform. It's just an alphabet that looks like cuneiform.
This essay is four times longer than the Samalian corpus.
Maybe it doesn't matter whether 𐎄𐎐𐎛𐎍 is דניאל or Danel or דנאל or Daniel. It's still the same name.
The same gods, El and Baal Hadad, are important gods in the Ugarit corpus! And this is coming four hundred years after the fall of Ugarit.
posts.key_insight: The modern name Daniel may represent an unbroken 3,400-year chain of cultural transmission, surviving the Bronze Age Collapse through shared Canaanite religious traditions and later preserved by the name's theological importance in Christianity.

posts.summary: The post warns against overusing Alloy's 'fact' keyword to constrain specification models. While facts prevent the model checker from generating certain states, this can backfire when you need to verify that your software correctly handles invalid inputs. The author argues that 'making illegal states unrepresentable' works well in programming but creates problems in specification because specs must model both the software and its environment. Using predicates instead of facts keeps constraints explicit and testable. Facts should be reserved for narrowing problem scope, eliminating uninteresting cases, optimization, or expressing relationships Alloy can't handle natively.
key_points: Facts make states unrepresentable, which prevents testing that your software correctly rejects those states
Specifications must model both the machine (your software) and the world (the environment), unlike regular programming where MISU is always good
Predicates are preferable to facts because they're explicit, locally scoped, and can be selectively applied

Legitimate uses for facts include narrowing problem scope, eliminating fundamentally uninteresting cases, symmetry breaking for optimization, and expressing type constraints Alloy can't handle natively
The core tension: you need error states to exist in your model to verify your program handles them correctly
money_quotes: If you cannot represent the illegal state, you cannot represent the world creating an illegal state that your software needs to handle.
Constraints are dangerous because you need error states in order to check that your program avoids error states.
Normally in programming languages, 'making illegal states unrepresentable' is a good thing. But specification covers both the software you are writing and the environment the software is running in.
These kinds of nonsensical situations arise often when we're specifying, because we have an intent of what the system should be but don't explicitly encode it.
Instead of facts, you want predicates. Then you can test for the predicate being true or check that it's necessary to get other properties. Make the constraint explicit instead of implicit.
'No self-deps' is a perfectly reasonable fact if we're only specifying the package installer and something else is responsible for validating the manifests.
posts.key_insight: In formal specification, making illegal states unrepresentable through facts prevents you from verifying that your software correctly handles those illegal states when they occur in the real world.

posts.summary: Hillel Wayne applies Clausewitz's military concept of 'friction' to software development, arguing that the gap between theoretical simplicity and practical difficulty is a fundamental, unavoidable aspect of building software. He catalogs diverse sources of friction—from API changes and bugs to sick kids and stolen laptops—and notes that friction compounds: two setbacks are more than twice as bad as one. The post explores how friction accumulates over time and scope, and how attempts to address it can create new friction. Wayne concludes with open questions about categorizing friction, learning from other fields, and whether individual awareness of friction provides value.
key_points: Friction is the difference between how simple software development appears in theory and how difficult it is in practice
Sources of friction are diverse and impossible to fully catalogue—technical, social, environmental, and organizational
Friction compounds non-linearly: two problems are more than twice as bad as one because systems lose resilience
Addressing friction can create new friction, like fixing a security alert with a backwards-incompatible upgrade
Friction matters more over large time horizons and scopes simply because more things can go wrong
money_quotes: Everything is very simple in war, but the simplest thing is difficult.
Friction compounds with itself: two setbacks are more than twice as bad as one setback.
A client changes what they want after development.
Friction is inevitable and impossible to fully remove. I don't think it's possible to even fully anticipate.
Addressing friction can also create other sources of friction.
Word changes every font to wingdings. (This is a real thing)
posts.key_insight: Software development friction—the accumulated gap between theoretical simplicity and practical difficulty—is inevitable, compounds non-linearly, and fighting it can create new friction.

posts.summary: This post presents a technique for composing large TLA+ specifications using state machines and a 'Sync' operator. The traditional approach to composing specs requires manually stitching together actions, which becomes unwieldy for large specifications. Wayne's solution uses TLA+'s ability to treat primed variables as both assignments and constraints, allowing a central state machine to guard and trigger transitions across independent components. The technique involves writing an abstract state machine for the core component, keeping other components 'open' (not fully self-contained), and using refinement to verify correctness. While more complex than simple specs warrant, this approach scales better for large specifications that need independent development.
key_points: TLA+ primed variables can serve as both assignments and constraints, enabling one spec to constrain another's transitions after the fact
The Sync operator acts as glue between independently developed specs, adding guards and side-effects to state transitions without modifying the original components
Specs can be written 'open' (incomplete) by making them stuttering-invariant only on internal variables, allowing external specs to drive their shared state
Refinement properties are transitive: if the composed system refines a component spec, all the component's verified properties carry over automatically
This approach trades upfront complexity for better maintainability and scalability compared to conventional composition that requires manually interlacing actions
money_quotes: Composing two specs can be as much work as writing them in the first place.
We can use x' to both assign the next value to x and constrain what the next value can be.
This is just a state machine! Constraints on transitions are just guard clauses and assignments on transitions are just effects.
Our worker doesn't need to know this. We can develop them independently and rely on the Sync to correctly compose their behaviors.
In TLA+, refinements are transitive.
The point of composing specs at all is so we can work on them independently.
This is a powerful technique, but also one that takes experience and adds complexity.
posts.key_insight: By leveraging TLA+'s dual interpretation of primed variables as both assignments and constraints, you can compose large specifications through a central Sync operator that guards and triggers transitions without modifying the original components.

posts.summary: Hillel Wayne introduces the concept of 'toolbox languages' - programming languages optimized for quickly solving problems without third-party dependencies. He showcases five personal favorites beyond Python: AutoHotKey for GUI automation, J for terse array computations, Frink for dimensional analysis with units, Raku for scripting with powerful syntactic features, and Picat for logic programming with constraint solving. The post argues that good toolbox languages prioritize speed of writing over other concerns, with terseness, rich builtins, and good REPLs being key characteristics.
key_points: Toolbox languages excel at solving problems quickly without requiring package installations - they're self-contained and immediately useful
Different toolbox languages serve different niches: AutoHotKey for GUI automation, J for math, Frink for unit-aware calculations, Raku for scripting, Picat for constraint/logic problems
Speed of writing is the primary criterion - a toolbox language should be faster than Python for its domain, achieved through terse syntax and extensive builtins
Good toolbox languages have smooth on-ramps (easy to start) but this often conflicts with terseness and power
The space of 'effects and automation' toolbox languages is underexplored compared to computation-focused ones
money_quotes: A toolbox language is a programming language that's good at solving problems without requiring third party packages.
Autohotkey is basically 'shell scripting for GUIs'. Just a fantastic tool to smooth over using unprogrammable applications.
Really good at doing arithmetic on arrays, hair-pullingly frustrating at doing anything with strings or structured data.
Raku (née Perl 6) is a *really* weird language filled to the brim with dark magic. It's very powerful and also very easy to screw up.
The `planner` module. I **love** the `planner` module. It is my best friend.
A toolbox language should be really, REALLY fast to write. At the very least, faster than Python.
If you can do something in a single line, you can throw it in a REPL.
Having to install a third-party package bothers me.
posts.key_insight: The best programming languages for quick problem-solving aren't necessarily the best general-purpose languages - specialized 'toolbox' languages that prioritize terseness and rich builtins can dramatically outperform Python for specific domains.

posts.summary: Hillel Wayne announces a temporary pause in blog writing to focus on completing his book 'Logic for Programmers'. He expects to resume blogging in early 2025. He notes that his weekly newsletter will continue during this hiatus.
key_points: Blog is on hiatus while the author focuses on writing 'Logic for Programmers'
Expected return is early 2025
Weekly newsletter continues uninterrupted
money_quotes: All of my budgeted blogwriting time is going to Logic for Programmers.
posts.key_insight: Sometimes you have to pause one project to focus on completing another.

posts.summary: Hillel Wayne adapts the Hierarchy of Controls (HoC) from workplace safety to software engineering, using his own experience of accidentally dropping a production database as a case study. The hierarchy ranks controls from most to least effective: elimination, substitution, engineering controls, administrative controls, and PPE. He argues that software is particularly well-suited for engineering controls since all interfaces are constructed rather than natural, making it easier to build in safeguards. The post notes that controls can themselves introduce new hazards and should be considered holistically. PPE translates poorly to software contexts since the framework was designed to protect humans from machines, not machines from humans.
key_points: The Hierarchy of Controls ranks prevention strategies: elimination > substitution > engineering controls > administrative controls > PPE
Engineering controls are embedded in systems and require effort to break, while administrative controls require effort to follow—a crucial distinction for reliability
Software's artificial interfaces make engineering controls cheaper and more practical than in physical systems, where natural interfaces default to allowing harm
The 'full name' confirmation box is stronger than y/N prompts because muscle memory works against accidental confirmation rather than enabling it
Controls themselves can introduce new hazards—strict access policies might slow incident response, too many alerts cause fatigue
money_quotes: In software, all interfaces are constructed and artificial: hazards come from us adding capabilities to do harm.
Engineering controls can fail, substitutions cannot.
It takes effort to break the engineering control, while it takes effort to follow the administrative control.
Essential hazards cannot be eliminated, but inessential hazards can.
The safety of the whole system must be considered holistically: local improvements in safety can cause problems elsewhere.
Software can be modified much more quickly and cheaply than physical systems can, which makes engineering controls more effective.
posts.key_insight: Software's constructed interfaces make engineering controls unusually cost-effective compared to physical systems, so we should bias toward building safety into systems rather than relying on training and procedures.

posts.summary: This post presents a JavaScript parsing puzzle where the expression 'x = 1' followed by 'x' and '--> 0' unexpectedly prints 1. The trick is that '-->' at the beginning of a line is a valid comment token in JavaScript, a legacy feature preserved from the days when developers wrapped scripts in HTML comments to hide them from older browsers that didn't understand the script tag. This hack was standardized in ECMAScript 2015 to avoid breaking old websites, demonstrating how backward compatibility concerns shape language design.
key_points: The --> token at the start of a line begins a comment in JavaScript, making it equivalent to //
This syntax exists because early web developers wrapped JavaScript in HTML comments for graceful degradation in browsers that predated script tag support
The ECMAScript standardization committee codified this hack in 2015 rather than break existing websites
<!-- can appear anywhere in a line as a comment, but --> only works at line start to avoid ambiguity with decrement operators
money_quotes: At the beginning of a line (and only at the beginning of a line), --> starts a comment.
It's a legacy hack.
Old browsers would parse the content as an HTML comment and ignore it, new browsers would parse the content as JavaScript and execute it.
Since some old websites still have the hack and the standardization committee will never, ever break the web, they added <!-- and --> as legal comment tokens to the 2015 standard.
posts.key_insight: JavaScript's bizarre --> comment syntax exists because the web's commitment to backward compatibility means even temporary hacks from 1995 get standardized forever.

posts.summary: Hillel Wayne offers a guide to video games for non-gamers, noting that despite gaming being a $500 billion industry, many people have never played because games assume existing 'vocabulary' of conventions. He proposes four criteria for good introductory games: no special hardware required, no assumed gaming knowledge, cultural significance, and personal verification that non-gamers enjoyed them. He then recommends four games—Baba is You (puzzle), Stardew Valley (farming sim), The Case of the Golden Idol (deduction), and Balatro (roguelike)—explaining each game's mechanics, appeal, and place in gaming history.
key_points: Video games are highly inaccessible to newcomers because 'big budget games' assume players already know gaming conventions and vocabulary
Good introductory games should run on basic hardware, require no prior gaming knowledge, be culturally significant, and have proven appeal to non-gamers
Puzzle games that create 'insight moments'—where something seems impossible until it suddenly becomes trivial—are particularly effective for new players
Games can be slow-paced and 'cozy' rather than violent or intense, as demonstrated by Stardew Valley's influence on the farming sim genre
Deduction games like The Case of the Golden Idol showcase how games can tell stories that only work because of the interactive medium
money_quotes: It would surprise me if a friend has never watched a movie or listened to music, but it's pretty normal for a friend to have never played a video game.
The problem is that games are highly inaccessible. To most people, games are 'big budget games', which are targeted at people who already have played lots of games.
A good puzzle game is designed around the 'insights', where you realize that something you never considered is possible within the game rules.
It shows that games don't have to be violent or intense to be good. Exceptional games can be slow-paced, relaxing, and ground themselves in everyday concerns.
Golden Idol works because of its interactivity. You learn the story by opening drawers and rifling through purses.
We know Balatro is going to inspire a new generation of roguelikes but we don't entirely know what they'll look like. And that's really exciting to me!
posts.key_insight: Gaming's accessibility problem isn't about complexity—Stardew Valley is more intricate than many 'complicated' games—but about whether the complexity is introduced in an approachable way that doesn't assume prior gaming literacy.

posts.summary: This post traces the origins of algebraic data types (AlgDTs) from John McCarthy's 1961 paper through their development in both imperative and functional programming traditions. McCarthy first defined the concepts of Cartesian products and direct unions (sum types) along with their algebraic properties like distributivity and associativity. The ideas influenced Tony Hoare and Rod Burstall, who developed them independently in different directions. While sum types disappeared from imperative languages due to Pascal and C's influence, they flourished in functional programming through ML, HOPE, and Miranda, eventually gaining features like tags, exhaustiveness checking, and pattern matching that define modern AlgDTs.
key_points: McCarthy's 1961 paper established both sum and product types along with their algebraic properties (associativity, distributivity) from the very beginning, even without using modern terminology
The terms come from set theory: product types from Cartesian products, sum types from disjoint unions, both named for their cardinality relationships
Sum types split into two traditions: Hoare influenced imperative languages (calling them 'discriminated unions'), while Burstall influenced functional programming
Pascal and C prevented sum types from gaining traction in imperative programming for decades
HOPE (1980) introduced pattern matching to functional programming, and VAX ML added tagged sums and products together for the first time
money_quotes: It doesn't matter if you're the first to have an idea if nobody else hears you have it.
All of these properties of 'algebraic data types' were known from the start, even if they weren't called that yet.
Pascal did not have sum types because Wirth thought they were less flexible than untagged unions.
After this point sum types disappear from imperative programming.
Burstall is incredibly frustrating to research.
If the programmer attempts to convert a discriminated union value back to a type from which it did not originate, this is a serious programming error.
This is the best I could do with the time and resources I had available.
posts.key_insight: The algebraic properties that give algebraic data types their name—associativity, distributivity, cardinality relationships—were all present in McCarthy's foundational 1961 paper, decades before the terminology was standardized.

posts.summary: Hillel Wayne shares a collection of Z3 SMT solver examples that were cut from his book Logic for Programmers. He walks through increasingly complex uses: solving systems of equations, proving no four distinct positive integers share both sum and product, optimizing financial contributions, reverse engineering RNG parameters, proving mathematical theorems, and modeling stock trading with Z3 arrays. He explains why most examples didn't make the book's final cut—they either weren't practical enough, required too much background explanation, or weren't the right tool for the job—and describes the three examples he ultimately chose.
key_points: Z3 is an SMT solver that can handle constraints involving integers, reals, arrays, regexes, and quantified expressions, making it far more versatile than simple equation solvers
SMT arrays are actually infinite key-value maps without inherent bounds, which can cause surprising behavior if you forget to constrain index ranges
You can prove theorems by asking Z3 to find counterexamples to the negation—if it returns unsat, the theorem holds (though you must also handle the 'unknown' case)
Good pedagogical examples must be understandable to newcomers, look like practical problems, and demonstrate that SMT solvers are the right tool—most toy examples fail at least one criterion
Z3's optimization is dramatically slower than dedicated constraint solvers or even hand-written Python binary search, limiting its practical use for optimization problems
money_quotes: You're supposed to learn how to do solve this as a system of equations, but if you want to cheat yourself out of an education you can have Z3 solve this for you.
An SMT ("Satisfiability Modulo Theories") solver is a constraint solver that understands math and basic programming concepts.
SMT operations must be total for all possible inputs, meaning Select(A, i) must return a value no matter what i is.
This means Z3 can find functions that satisfy constraints!
Z3's core engine is in C++, and yet a hand-written Python binary search finds the optimal c about a 1000x faster!

They should be understandable to someone who is new to logic, they should look like a practical problem some readers might see in their jobs, and SMT solvers should be the right tool for solving them.
If you can convert a programming function to a set of Z3 equations you can prove that the function has expected properties.
posts.key_insight: Z3 is a remarkably versatile tool that can solve equations, prove theorems, and reverse engineer algorithms, but choosing good pedagogical examples requires balancing accessibility, practicality, and tool-appropriateness.
